225899652.review:
Outlast is great, but if you're not easily startled then the tension starts to wear off once you figure out how the enemies work and when/how the game will try and spook you. Outside of the camera battery and the sections where you have to hide, this is like two steps away from being a spooky walking simulator. 
Still, the environment is cool, the vibes are on-point, and the characters are great. I loved learning more about the crazies that were trying to kill me. The overall plot started to lose me near the end but that's nothing new for the horror genre. 
It's crazy that there are STILL games trying to recreate the magic of this short little game, and most of them fail.

225779237.review:
Outlast is a great game and for those that enjoy being scared they're going to love it. Unfortunately I made it only halfway before realizing I'm not the right person for this kind of experience.

Red Barrels went all in on helplessness as a design philosophy and never blinked. You are a journalist, you have a camera, you cannot fight back, and the asylum you've broken into is full of things that want to find you in the dark. It works exactly as intended which is precisely the problem for anyone who doesn't want their gaming sessions to feel like a cardiovascular event.

The camera night vision mechanic is genuinely clever. Battery management adds a layer of tension that pure darkness never could, and the decision of when to switch it on and burn through your supply versus navigating blind is a constant low grade anxiety that compounds everything else happening around you. 

The dev understood that the best horror is about what you think is there as much as what actually is.
The production quality sits well above what the budget suggests and the environmental storytelling scattered through Mount Massive Asylum rewards the kind of curiosity that terror makes very difficult to act on. There's a real story being told through documents and recordings for anyone brave enough to stop running long enough to find them.

\You are always running, always hiding, always listening for footsteps and breathing in the dark. If that loop sounds like fun you're going to have a genuinely great time. If it sounds like homework you're going to tap out around the halfway mark like I did

No shame in that. Some games just know exactly who they're for.

225771581.review:
Outlast is an absolute classic in the horror genre. When the game first released, I was an 11-year-old watching YouTubers play it, far too terrified to try it myself. Now, more than a decade later at 23, I was finally able to play through the entire game, proudly joining the 25% of players who have completed it.

Outlast contains many horror game elements most people would dread experiencing, such as: pitch blackness, jumpscares, a handheld light source which runs on batteries you must find and collect, and the sheer panic of running and hiding in lockers or underneath beds. This combination of elements truly solidifies the gameplay experience as a masterpiece within the genre.

However, having played Outlast for myself now, I have noticed a few areas Outlast is weak in:

- Audio Balance

By default, the volume of the background music and sound effects drowns out the voiced lines of the NPCs. Because I couldn't hear well, I struggled to keep up with the story. I eventually got fed up with this imbalance during Rudolf's long monologue near the end, opened the settings, and discovered I could have enabled subtitles. It was too late for me, but after playing, I read [url=https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=834854610]this guide[/url] which was somewhat useful. I also want to note that while I collected a handful of documents, many of them felt useless in actually explaining the lore.

- Low Enemy Model Variety 

There aren't a lot of different enemy models. You have standouts like Father Martin, the Doctor, the two gentlemen by the cells discussing who kills us first, and the patient strapped to the bed whose "boredom" the Doctor is relieving. But the rest are mostly the same huge, buff guys trying to rip you apart, or skinny ones with maces telling you to "back off."

- Too Many Empty Bathrooms

A few bathrooms had batteries in them, and a few others had some organs, but most were completely empty. It feels like a missed opportunity—the developers could have hidden a key in one of them or made an enemy jump out of a stall to keep you on your toes.

- Too Many Locked Doors

How many black steel cage doors as well as doors boarded from behind are there that are locked ? I dare you to count them all. I understand that it adds immersion to the horror experience, but it was  just too much. I feel like the developers outdid the amount of locked doors. I did, however, appreciate the few instances where the game actually lets you find an alternate route to a room with a locked door through another side of the room with an unlocked door. 

- Skipping Credits Causes PC To Be Unresponsive

Skipping the game credits caused the game to stay on an infinite loading screen. I couldn't open my task manager with Ctrl+ Esc + Shift or forcefully exit the game with Alt + F4. I had to force shut down my PC. Specs attached. 

Miles fan signing off with some screenshots I took: 

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3727871769
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3727875453
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3727876222

[h1] Overall Rating: 7/10 [/h1]

225452346.review:
Over a decade later, Outlast still holds up as one of the best horror games of all time.

Usually when you conquer a horror game and complete everything it has to offer, the scariness goes away. Not with this game though (for me anyway). After getting all of the achievements, learning every encounter inside-out, and beating the game at least 10 times, I still get scared upon repeat playthroughs. Maybe I’m a coward, I don’t know, but anytime I make it to a Chris Walker segment, my adrenaline gets fired up like Hell. The faint rattle of his chains in the distance, the sinister brass kicking in as you sneak away. Actual chills, bro.

The premise for the game is simple yet effective. You’re a journalist who’s been tipped off that something sinister is happening within a psychiatric hospital. You make your way inside the hospital only to be met with gory anarchy. A seemingly unending supply of dead patients and guards decorate every nook and cranny within the building. As you try to escape with your findings, you only sink deeper into the asylum’s madness like quicksand. In doing so, you meet the murderous culprits one by one. This is where you’ll find the main source of horror in this game: the encounters.

Running and hiding from psychos while juggling objectives required to escape is this game’s meat and potatoes. Getting caught by your aggressor is always a sure-fire adrenaline rush. Once your pursuer sees you, the game blasts (and borderline jumpscares you with) a heart pumping orchestral track. Grade-A, masterfully horrifying music that perfectly conveys the terror of running for your life. Whether or not you escape death is irrelevant. That feeling of raw fear as you sprint away from potential dismemberment. That primal, burning pit in your stomach shrieking in terror. That’s Outlast’s greatest strength in my opinion.

The graphics are phenomenal even by today’s standards, which is impressive given how old this game is. You can see the game’s age at points, but these moments are few and far between. My only real problem with the visuals is that Red Barrels reused a lot of the same patient and guard models ad nauseum. This breaks my immersion since multiple throwaway characters end up looking exactly the same, yet sounding and acting completely different. I’m not saying every single throwaway background character should’ve had a unique design. I doubt that would’ve been possible for budgetary reasons. But a few extra patient and guard models in the asset pool would’ve gone a long way for me.

Other than that small nitpick, Outlast is an amazing horror game. It’s a terrifying, infinitely replayable on-rails thrill ride. I’d recommend it to anyone looking to explore the darkest depths gaming has to offer.

225416531.review:
I got this game in the first place after playing Outlast Trials a bunch, a game I very much fell in love with, leading me to get highly curious about its predecessors, which led me to buying and playing through this.
For a game which is only around 5 hours worth of gameplay, it’s so incredibly in depth and fun. Outlast is definitely the best 1st person solo horror game I’ve ever played. First of all, the graphics in this game are really good. Combined with the games design and the map, it all adds up to a really immersive experience. The story is another huge positive in this game, I find that in a lot of horror games they tend to stick to a similar lore format but Outlast is clearly different from the rest, the collectables you can get during gameplay, as well as little minor dialogue and contextual cues hidden around are really good additions while trying to figure out the story.
Outlast’s pacing and gameplay mechanics itself are also top notch ; The game’s introductory of needing to use a camera to see in the dark is genius while also being super stressful, while the pacing is constantly making you unnerved and worried about what’s to come. The soundtrack is yet again another aspect I really like about this, also contributing to what makes a very high quality immersive experience, really well done.
I’d also like to mention how well done the character designs are. Both Chris Walker and Richard Trager were really quite disturbing, the way those character’s are handled are also really well done, I really liked their chase scenes and overall story revolving around those characters. The twins and priest were also very good as well, being very satisfactory pieces to the puzzle.
Finally, I’d like to mention how well done the ending is. It’s such a cool way to end the game in that really unique way, being such a pleasant surprise which makes me very eager to go down a lore-learning rabbit hole.
Overall I’d definitely say I highly enjoyed this horror game, so so good, an actually disturbing experience which I genuinely got really uncomfortable with multiple times, I highly recommend this fantastic masterpiece of a game. 9/10

225296357.review:
I had already played when I was younger, but I only decided to properly finish the 100% it recently. And honestly, even after all this time, the game still manages to create tension better than many modern horror games.

At first, the game throws you directly into chaos with almost no explanation. You quickly understand that you are completely defenseless and that your only options are to run, hide, or pray the enemy loses track of you. This creates a constant feeling of pressure throughout the entire experience. The camera mechanic with the night vision is simple, but it works perfectly and becomes one of the most iconic parts of the gameplay.

The progression is basically linear. You explore the asylum, activate objectives, find documents, and survive scripted encounters while learning more about what happened there. There are some moments where the game relies too much on trial-and-error chases, which can become frustrating sometimes, especially near the end. Still, the atmosphere and pacing carry the experience very well.

The sound design is probably one of the strongest aspects of the game. Every scream, footstep, or distant noise keeps you paranoid. The soundtrack itself is used less often, but when it appears it fits perfectly with the tension. Graphically, the game still looks good considering its age, mainly because the lighting and environment design do most of the work.

I didn’t encounter any major bugs during my playthrough. The AI can occasionally behave in weird ways, but nothing game-breaking. Getting 100% achievements is not very difficult, although completing the game on the highest difficulty without reloading can be stressful if you are not patient.

In the end, the game is still one of the best modern horror experiences if your focus is atmosphere and tension rather than combat. It may not be very replayable after the first run, but the first experience is definitely memorable.

224896279.review:
Outlast is not just a horror game—it’s a completely new kind of horror experience that changed how I see the genre. The idea of playing as a cameraman—a journalist investigating an abandoned asylum—was something I had never experienced before, and it turned out to be truly awesome and one of a kind.

From the moment you step into Mount Massive Asylum, the game doesn’t just try to scare you—it gets inside your head. This is pure psychological horror. The atmosphere is suffocating, the darkness feels alive, and every sound makes you question what’s coming next. I’m not exaggerating when I say this game was so, so, so scary that I couldn’t play it continuously. I would play for a few minutes, pause, take a breath, calm myself down… then go back in. Repeat. Over and over. It’s that intense.

What makes Outlast special is its core gameplay design. You are completely defenseless. No weapons, no fighting back—nothing. All you have is a camera. And that alone changes everything. Instead of confronting danger, you’re forced to hide, run, and survive. This creates a constant feeling of vulnerability that most horror games fail to achieve.

The camera mechanic itself is genius. Using night vision to navigate the darkness is both essential and terrifying. But the real brilliance lies in the battery system. Batteries are limited, and you’re always aware of how many you have left. Every step becomes a decision:

- Do I turn on night vision now?
- Should I save battery for later?
- What if I run out at the worst moment?
This constant resource management adds a layer of tension that never lets you relax.

And then… there’s that moment.
That chapter where you lose your camera.

Honestly, that part broke me. I had gotten so used to relying on the camera that losing it felt like losing my only sense of safety. The darkness becomes unbearable. You feel blind, exposed, and completely helpless. It was so intense that I actually stopped playing for a couple of days before coming back to continue. That alone shows how powerful this game is.

The story is also very well done. It’s not just about cheap scares—it builds a disturbing narrative through documents, recordings, and the environment itself. The asylum feels like it has a history, and uncovering what happened there keeps you engaged beyond just the fear factor.

The sound design deserves special mention. Every footstep, every distant scream, every sudden noise is crafted to keep you on edge. Combined with the visuals and lighting, it creates an experience that feels deeply immersive and genuinely terrifying.

I also have to give credit to the enemy design. The characters you encounter are not just random threats—they are disturbing, memorable, and add to the overall horror in a meaningful way. Each encounter feels unique and unpredictable.

And then there’s the DLC—Outlast: Whistleblower. This is not just extra content; it’s a fully realized extension of the main game. It adds meaningful depth to the story, showing events from another perspective and filling in important gaps. It doesn’t feel like a side story—it feels essential.

What makes Whistleblower even better is that it pushes the horror even further. The tension is higher, the situations feel more extreme, and some moments are honestly more disturbing than anything in the base game. It keeps the same core mechanics—running, hiding, managing your camera—but raises the stakes in a way that feels natural and terrifying at the same time.

The environments in the DLC are just as strong, if not more intense. You revisit familiar places but see them in a completely different light, which adds a fresh layer of fear. And just when you think you’ve adapted to the game’s style, it throws something new at you that completely breaks your comfort zone.

What I respect the most is that the DLC doesn’t try to be different for the sake of it—it stays true to what made Outlast great, while expanding on it in all the right ways.
What Red Barrels achieved here is incredible. They didn’t just make a horror game—they created a new style of horror experience. One that focuses on helplessness, tension, and psychological fear rather than combat.
The level design plays a huge role in this. The asylum is not just a setting—it feels like a character itself. Narrow hallways, locked doors, hidden paths, vents, and rooms to hide in… everything is designed to keep you tense. You’re constantly scanning your surroundings, memorizing escape routes, and preparing for the worst. And when the worst actually happens, it feels real.

What I also appreciated is how the game avoids becoming repetitive, even though the core mechanics are simple. Yes, you mostly run, hide, and manage your camera—but the situations constantly change. Different enemies behave differently, environments shift, and the pressure never feels exactly the same twice. The game keeps evolving just enough to keep you on edge.

The chase sequences deserve special mention. They are some of the most heart-pounding moments I’ve experienced in gaming. You’re running blindly, turning corners, slamming doors, hoping you don’t make a wrong move. There’s no feeling like it—pure panic. And when you barely escape and hide successfully, the relief is incredible.

Another powerful element is the feeling of isolation. You are completely alone. No help, no backup, no weapons—just you and whatever horrors are lurking in the dark. This isolation amplifies the fear and makes every encounter feel more personal and intense.

Even the small details add to the experience:

- The way the camera flickers when batteries are low
- The heavy breathing when you hide in lockers
- The subtle environmental storytelling in each room

All of these elements come together to create something that feels carefully crafted and deeply immersive.

Overall, Outlast is an unforgettable journey into fear. It’s creepy, intense, and brilliantly designed. It may not be perfect, but what it does, it does exceptionally well.

My rating is 9/10.
If you’re a fan of horror, this is not just a recommendation—it’s a must-play.

224641555.review: Honestly, words can’t even describe how insane this game is. Outlast is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ amazing and easily the most terrifying experience I’ve ever had. What makes it work is that everything, the atmosphere, the music, the sound, is just perfect. It doesn’t rely on that lazy horror trope where the protagonist is some tough guy with a gun dropping F-bombs every two seconds while blasting the villains. In this game, you are actually helpless. You feel like you’re in real danger every single second because your only choice is to run or hide. That constant fear of being hunted makes it its own unique game; I don't think I've ever played another game that made me feel this genuinely scared. And don't even get me started on the DLC. Whistleblower is probably even better than the main game. It feels like the perfect finish to the story, and the fact that you get to see what the asylum was like right before the "outbreak" is fantastic. It adds so much context to the madness. If you want a game that actually gets your heart racing, stop reading this and go buy it. Highly recommend.

224122613.review:
After 73 hours, I can confidently say this is one of the purest horror experiences out there.

Outlast doesn’t rely on cheap jumpscares alone—it builds a constant sense of dread through its atmosphere, sound design, and the feeling of being completely defenseless. The fact that you can’t fight back changes everything. You’re not a hero here… you’re prey.

What really makes this game stand out is its psychological horror. It’s not just about monsters, but about corruption, abandonment, and the disturbing reality behind Mount Massive Asylum. Every note you find and every corridor you explore adds to that feeling of something deeply wrong.

The night vision mechanic is simple but genius, forcing you to manage your resources while pushing forward into the dark. And even after many hours, the game still manages to make you feel tense.

If you enjoy horror that makes you feel vulnerable and unsettled—not just scared for a moment—this is absolutely worth playing.

Highly recommended.

223397178.review:
Outlast is a short video game, that is only extended by playing on higher difficulties and through experimentation. That is not to say it is a bad game, by any stretch of the imagination. It is a modern staple in the catalogue of good horror. While its pattern of gameplay may feel repetitive, it cleverly uses suspense to force out early scares upon your first playthrough. Many jumpscares that I knew were coming--either having witnessed them through a Let's Play or myself on console--still caught a jump or gasp from me. It is a timeless classic that brought back a focus to survival horror, and not the kind in which you can simply fight back. 

  The only major downside, aside from a limited AI that can eventually be exploited, is the game's immersive design can make it difficult to figure out what you're looking for. While these items do highlight themselves, there is one in particular near the end of the game that can be overlooked--especially during a chase sequence. I've also found the AI can sometimes rubberband and catch up to you when they are generally supposed to be no faster than your sprint. The latter is a very specific issue, and only occurs if you take too long or get caught on geometry. The former is also only a major issue near the end of the game, since most of the game saves chase sequences for before or after the scavenger hunt. Furthermore, there is a document that can better explain that you must return to a specific spot to proceed, so as long as you find that before the chase sequence, it may never be an issue of trial and error.

  Overall, I recommend the first Outlast to any fan of horror. At least play the first before experiencing the superior Whistleblower DLC or the multiplayer Outlast Trials which both revolve around the same antagonistic group of the franchise--Outlast [1] gives a simultaneous story that is connected to the DLC, and greatly introduces you to contexts of the evils that you will experience in Outlast Trials.

222756278.review:
Naked and afraid... and bald. 

I bought this game in 2015 with the Whistleblower DLC. I remember getting stuck and being too jumpy to progress. I forgot about it after uninstalling (object permanence, babbyyyy). I spent the next 11 years developing a hyper fixation on horror mainly in movies. 
Boom, it's today (2026). I finished the game and DLC and have some words. 
Likes:
-This game feels like the first Saw movie, it's nostalgic, sets some precedents for the genre (looking at you camcorder)
-Jumpscares are still fun and will always bring people back to the game
-Gritty, grotesque, and intriguing 
-The graphics hold up well enough for 2013
-The DLC and game are equally matched 

Dislikes:
-The themes have NOT aged well
-Everyone's nakey (this could be a like)and it feels campy instead of scary
-There's an ungodly amount of sexual assault/talk of that felt unnecessary (I kept saying "here we go again..." instead of "ahhhhhhhhhh")
-This game kinda feels like Wolfenstein/Disney movie, yeah you lose some fingers and lots of blood and you fall off/get thrown off/blown off every ledge, get stabbed, shot, etc. but girl, get up. Time to be saved by another miracle and go find another false exit. Why not one more chase sequence. 
-Most of the documents aren't really lore/plot related, they are just there for a light read

Teenage me would have recommended this game because it scared me. Now that I'm ancient, I can see all the problems without the adrenaline goggles on.
So if you want some jumpscares and a repetitive gritty story, go for it. Also, get the DLC if you need a refill.

No matter how many years pass, this game will remain the best in its genre for me.

 Watching a walkthrough several years ago and playing it now, I feel the same horror and fear. Maybe you can even remember when you first heard about this game and how Outlast evoked new emotions and impressions of this genre in so many people.

Not only is there a great plot and the fact that you yourself feel the horror the main character goes through, but the whole aesthetic of a crazy hospital, the conversations of crazy patients, the story itself is so crazy and good that it evokes admiration and you begin to wonder if everything is okay with you, that you like it. Not to mention, I'm sure some people still think Gluskin or Trager is Hear Me Out (I'm with you guys, I'm with you)

Nowadays, there are already a huge number of horror films and films, especially about psychiatric hospitals, but the mark the first Outlast left on me will never compare to any other horror and the emotions I still experience to this day

222585974.review:
Is it perfect? No.
Is it very predictable and repetitive at some points? Yes.
Is there some absolutely ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ crazy and unsettling stuff going on the whole time? Yes.
Does everything make sense? No.
Is there way too much blood everywhere? Yes.
Do you see a man’s genitals at one point? Yes.
Do you almost get your own junk sawed off on a table while a guy basically says he’s going to make a bride out of you? Also yes.

And somehow, even with all that, I’d still recommend it, especially with the DLC. Outlast is one of those horror games that is not flawless, but it is still memorable as hell. The gameplay can get repetitive since you are mostly just running, hiding, and using your camera, and after a while some of the chase sections start to feel predictable. But the atmosphere, the gore, the insanity, and the sheer uneasiness of the whole experience is great. 

I actually liked the DLC a little more than the base game. It adds a lot to the story and makes the overall experience better, especially if you play it right after finishing the main game.

For a 2013 horror game, this still holds up really well. If you like intense, disturbing horror and you can handle some trial and error gameplay, I’d say it is absolutely worth picking up on sale.

8.5/10

222310075.review:
This is one of those games from the "golden age" of YouTube, I guess you could say; I remember many of today's big YouTubers playing this back in 2013, such as Pewdiepie (the one I watched the most). 

Honestly the game still holds up very nicely, both in graphics and gameplay; the atmosphere is also incredibly eerie and scary, especially in darker sections. 
I also feel like there's a good speedrun potential after you've played it a while (if you're into that stuff); to be honest, it's not the longest or most demanding game once you get the hang of it; it's just on point, which is better in my opinion, since it nails everything a horror game should aim for and doesn't get boring by being too lengthy either. Anyways, try to enjoy it as much as possible on your first playthrough! For me at least it stopped being "scawy" after playing it a few times, but that doesn't mean I won't be coming back to play it in the future, I mean I came back because of Outlast Trials, for example lol (it's a really good game as well).

You should definitely give it a try if you haven't already, and in case you have... replay it! Btw there's a few tweaks you can do (and also should) in the game files: 
The first one is the FOV, the default is quite narrow, even more so if you use the camera. You can search on Google where to change it (both the camera's fov and normal fov, since it's different).
The second one that was a bit annoying for me at least is the mouse acceleration, and yes, you also need to change that in the game's files.
There are many other things you can change in the game files, that actually make the game look better, but I believe in you to manage it in case you're interested.
If you perchance have any problems with changing those things, leave a message and I'll try helping you out!

Anyway, I guess I'm already rambling a bit; just play the game, and thanks for reading! [i]-if you actually did read...[/i]

222141264.review:
Overall the game's definitely a classic that draws in various classic horror elements like body horror, extreme gore and chase scenes. Despite this, I feel that a lot of the elements in the basegame were lacking; mostly how repetitive it was: most of the levels were a case of memorising the enemies' patrol patterns, grabbing whatever item(s) you needed to unlock something, and then something unfortunate happens. And even within the levels it's difficult to know where you're supposed to go, or where these items are, because there's nothing to indicate the location, apart from blood close to the end. Because of this, the progression feels extremely slow, and any task that you complete has VERY little payoff, especially compared to the hassle of somehow passing the enemies that spawn in the same hallway or doorway you need to go through. Even the horror element as mentioned at the beginning was underwhelming, since it mostly consisted of shock value based off piles of bodies and guts on the floor, as well as the odd jumpscare. After only an hour into the game, I was already desensitised to this because of how regularly these were used. I understand that the game was made during a time when the standards for indie horror games were lower, but I wanted to write this to express my pain whilst playing this.

In saying this, I do, however, recommend Whistleblower. I love Eddie Gluskin. And the DLC itself I guess.

222048156.review:
What an absolutely phenomenal horror game. I was pleasantly surprised by how great of an experience it was. The atmosphere is incredible, the game isn't loaded with cheap jumpscares, the enemies are intimidating and interesting, and the level designs are incredible. Exploring the areas was so enjoyable, I wouldn't even mind if it was just a walking sim. The story is good, not great, but not bad or disappointing either. The gameplay is incredibly smooth. You have sections where you have to run from enemies and hide, and while this may seem boring, it is handled incredibly. Each enemy encounter feels intense and the chase sequances are extremely fast-paced. I say this as a hater of the hide-and-seek mechanic in horror games, because they generally tend to feel slow and repetitive. In Outlast it's done incredibly well.

My only downside is that it's not THAT scary, it's scary enough to appreciate, but not scary enough to hesitate progressing. If you're desensitized to horror games (like I am), you'll still find it relatively scary, though. It's definitely satisfying in the scary department. Just not Visage levels of scary, if you know what I mean. It's a great starter horror game, in my opinion. The Whistleblower DLC is definitely scarier than the main game, and also a must if you want the full Outlast experience.

Personally this works for me, and I had enough of what I wanted. I won't be playing Outlast 2 or the Outlast Trials, because I honestly don't think a sequal was ever necessary. I'd rather preserve and limit my experience with the first game and its DLC. 

Game: 8/10
Scariness: 7.5/10

221254167.review:
Red Barrels’ Outlast remains one of the most infamous names in survival horror—and for good reason. Few games manage to blend tension, helplessness, and visceral terror quite this effectively. It’s brutal, grotesque, and downright exhausting in the best (and sometimes worst) ways. The sheer amount of gore and creative executions makes every chase sequence stick in your mind, particularly if you played it young enough for the nightmares to take root for days afterward

From a gameplay design standpoint, Outlast thrives on vulnerability. The camera-as-flashlight mechanic still feels bold—a clever way to limit your vision while forcing you into the darkness. But the battery system that fuels it hasn’t aged well. Run out, and you’re done for, which can make some sections feel more like resource punishment than horror immersion. The courtyard sequence, in particular, is where this design frustrates most; pitch black, open-ended, and nearly impossible to navigate without running in circles

Narratively, Outlast starts off strong with its grounded psychological horror—the asylum, the deranged inmates, the slow unraveling of sanity. Unfortunately, the final act slips into a more sci-fi direction that feels less focused and far less terrifying. The shift toward laboratory horror and scientific experimentation breaks the primal fear built earlier. It’s still serviceable, but nowhere near as memorable or cohesive as the main body of the game

Still, for all its rough edges, Outlast delivers what it promises: a relentless, blood-soaked nightmare that leaves you tense until the end. It defined a generation of horror streamers for a reason, and even now, its first hour remains among the most chilling in gaming

No technical issues for Mac version(M2-2022), other than its usual overheating after one hour

221210145.review:
[hr][/hr]
[h2]✔ RECOMMENDED[/h2]
[hr][/hr]

[h1]OUTLAST REVIEW[/h1]

Horror games often rely on cheap jumpscares, but Outlast does something different—it makes you feel completely helpless. From the very beginning, you’re thrown into Mount Massive Asylum with no weapons, no way to fight back, and no safety. Your only options are to run, hide, and survive.

[hr][/hr]
[h2]Overview[/h2]

The atmosphere is where the game truly shines. Dark corridors, disturbing environments, and constant tension make you feel like you're never alone. The camcorder mechanic is simple but genius—night vision is your only way to see, but it also makes everything more intense.

Sound design is easily one of the strongest parts of the game. Every noise matters—footsteps, screams, and distant sounds keep you on edge the entire time.

[hr][/hr]
[h2]PC Requirements 💻[/h2]

Outlast is scary enough for your soul, not your GPU. This game can run on almost anything—from a potato PC to a high-end rig.

[b]Potato PC 🥔[/b] → Can run it, fear intact
[b]Good PC 💻[/b] → Runs smoothly with better visuals
[b]High-end 🔥[/b] → Max settings, full horror immersion

[hr][/hr]
[h2]Pros ✅[/h2]

Incredible atmosphere 🌑
Amazing sound design 🎧
Unique “no combat” gameplay 🏃‍♂️
Constant tension 😰
Simple but effective mechanics 🎮

[hr][/hr]
[h2]Cons ❌[/h2]

Gameplay can feel repetitive 🔁
Story is not very deep 📖

221196103.review:
Outlast is a first-person survival horror game developed by Red Barrels, and it stands as one of the most terrifying horror experiences in gaming.

You play as investigative journalist Miles Upshur, who explores Mount Massive Asylum after receiving a mysterious tip. From the moment you step inside, the game creates an intense atmosphere filled with fear, tension, and uncertainty.

The biggest strength of *Outlast* is its atmosphere. The asylum is dark, disturbing, and claustrophobic, with sound design that constantly keeps you on edge—distant screams, footsteps, and sudden noises make every moment feel dangerous.

A unique gameplay mechanic is the use of a night-vision camcorder. Since most areas are pitch dark, you rely on it to see—but it consumes batteries, which are limited. This creates constant tension, forcing you to manage resources carefully.

Unlike many horror games, *Outlast* doesn’t give you weapons. You can’t fight back—you can only run, hide, or escape. This makes encounters with enemies much more intense and panic-inducing.

However, the game isn’t for everyone. The heavy focus on stealth and running may feel repetitive over time, and some players might find the experience too stressful. Also, while the story is interesting, it’s mostly told through notes and recordings rather than direct interaction.

Overall, **Outlast** delivers pure horror with minimal distractions. It’s intense, immersive, and unforgettable for fans of the genre.

Overall Rating: 9/10
A must-play if you enjoy survival horror that keeps you constantly on edge.

220878477.review:
Outlast is a terrifying, gory horror game from 2013 that tasks you, the main character Mr. Journalist Forgothisname, to investigate an Asylum and a connected corporation. Armed with a trusty camcorder and a whole load of replacement underwear, you venture inside and things go very wrong very quickly.

Outlast is very scary in the way that a haunted house is scary. It can be terrifying, but in a predictable, almost over-the-top kind of way. Most of that is due to the high amount of jumpscares, which are the worst form of trying to scare someone. Sure, it got a rise out of me plenty of times, but the game uses it so often that it got annoying before I was even halfway through the game. 

While you're not getting jumpscared, you slowly make your way through the halls of the Asylum, finding batteries for your camcorder without which you can't see in the dark, recording events and finding documents as you keep investigating and exploring.

I feel like Outlast's primary form of scare, the jumpscares, almost hurt the game's main goal of being terrifying, because Outlast can be genuinely scary and tense even without them. The environmental storytelling is excellent, the sound design and music are hauntingly appropriate and a lot of the depicted scenarios and characters are seriously messed up. And before you can take it all in - oh, whoops! Another jumpscare. Sure, it was the early 2010s, where horror = sudden loud noise, but...I feel like leaning into the environmental storytelling more would've elevated the experience even higher.

The only real form of gameplay, aside from exploring, comes from sections where you have to find a key / switch / multiple switches / items while also dodging an enemy / multiple enemies trying to kill you. To do so, you can hide either under beds or in lockers, the only two places to hide in the whole game, or in shadows, which only works sometimes. Not only are these sections insanely repetitive as they happen very often throughout the games short runtime of 4.5-6 hours, but they are also the only time where the game isn't linear, often forcing you into dead ends and draining your camera battery. Often it's better to just die once you know where to go so your battery fills back up and you save time, which isn't very fun. Still, the great sound design makes these sections very tense and flexes the game's horror muscles - all without sudden loud noises!

Now this review so far sounds somewhat negative but honestly, I think Outlast is a pretty good time. Jumpscares and repetitive gameplay aside, I think the story the game tells and the environments and situations it depicts are both worth experiencing if you have the stomach for it and the DLC is a very good cap on the whole experience. Good for one playthrough with headphones in a few sessions late at night and just the right length too!

I didn’t watch any gameplay before beating it, and I recommend not looking up videos beforehand if you want the full experience—unless you’re completely stuck. This is about a game called Outlast 1.

220859001.review:
I've had this sat in my Steam Library since its release and only got round to playing through it this weekend. I enjoyed the experience, and I'm surprised that I didn't do it sooner. The atmosphere is outstanding, and is very reminiscent of titles like Manhunt; the graphics still look beautiful, too. The gameplay is simple and far more linear than other survival horror games, like Resident Evil and Silent Hill; you'll probably finish it in under six hours.

The game is very immersive, albeit limited in mechanics, with a heavy emphasis on stealth. Sometimes these sneaking/hiding mechanics work, and oftentimes they feel flimsy, as though the rules aren't necessarily concrete. This could be considered more realistic and engaging to some. 

What's really apparent is how this game was evidently the genesis for other modern first-person survival horror games such as PT, Alien Isolation, and Resident Evil 7; if you like any of those titles, you'll definitely enjoy what's on offer here.

220497835.review:
I think Outlast is the best single-player horror game I’ve played so far. The game does an amazing job of creating a very creepy atmosphere that constantly keeps you on edge. The jump scares are used well—not too often, but enough to surprise you and make your heart race. The sound design is also excellent; the footsteps, distant noises, and sudden sounds make the whole experience even more intense and immersive.

The gameplay mainly revolves around three things: sneaking, hiding, and running away. You often have to move carefully through dark environments while avoiding enemies. It becomes incredibly intense when you’re hiding in a locker or sneaking past someone who could catch you at any moment. There are also some really cool chase sequences where you have to run as fast as possible to escape enemies, which creates some of the most thrilling moments in the game.

The story is also super interesting. The plot is very well written and contains several great plot twists in the middle that keep you guessing about what’s really going on. The ending is especially cool and leaves a strong impression. It definitely makes you want to play the DLC and also try out the second game.

Overall, Outlast is an intense and memorable horror experience that I would highly recommend to anyone who enjoys single-player horror games.

219877288.review:
A pure tension filled experience that holds up as one of the best survival horror games ever. 

The graphics may be outdated, but the art design, lighting and audio makes the asylum a truly amazing horror setting. 

There's a good amount of mystery to unravel, which can enter some disturbing context, especially the DLC content which was a great extention. The villains here are super memorable with some great character writing. 

The enemy AI is easily abusable once you understand it, but the tight spaces make it less problematic than it could be. The focus on run and hide can get repetitive alongside a little too many lever pulling sections, but the game is smart enough to remain short and tight packed. 

Rating: 9.5 / 10

219858730.review:
Outlast
.
A nice horror game, great scene directing, creepy music and sounds.
The game amazingly introduces gameplay
"Outlast contains intense violence, gore, graphic sexual content, and strong language. Please enjoy.
You are Miles Upshur, an investigative reporter whose ambition is about to earn him an intimate tour of hell on earth. Always willing to risk digging into the stories no other journalist would dare investigate, you will seek out the dark secret at the heart of Mount Massive Asylum. Stay alive as long as you can, record everything. You are not a fighter; to navigate the horrors of Mount Massive and expose the truth, your only choices are to run, hide, or die."
.
Controls:
WASD - move
Ctrl - crouch
LMB - use
RMB - toggle camcorder
Shift - run
Space - jump
F - toggle night vision
Q, E - lean
Mouse wheel - Camera Zoom
R - reload batteries
Esc - escape menu
Tab - Reporter's Notebook
N - Show notes
J - Show Documents
.
Mechanics and features:
Before entering the asylum we read a note. The note is from a guy who worked there. The guy said that "Terrible things are happening there. Doctors talking about dream therapy going too deep, finding something that had been waiting for them in the mountain. People are being hurt and Murkoff is making money. It needs to be exposed."
We can open doors slowly and quickly.

219803904.review:
This game is just pure fear and panic the whole time, no fighting back, no hero moments - just you, a camera, and bad decisions.

Every hallway feels wrong, every sound makes you freeze for a second, and then suddenly someone is chasing you. Now you’re running with no plan, hiding in lockers, crawling under beds, slamming doors, hoping they don’t see you.

And when you think you got used to it, Whistleblower DLC comes in and makes everything worse; that DLC is another level. More disturbing, more intense, and way more chaotic. It barely gives you time to breathe, it’s just nonstop stress.

You’re not playing smart, you’re surviving on instinct. Run, hide, don’t look back.

This isn’t horror where you feel strong, this is horror where you feel small, hunted, and completely helpless.

Honestly one of the scariest gaming experiences I’ve had.

Recommended if you actually want to feel fear while playing 😭

219774663.review: I really enjoyed this game. The horror aspect was tense and really put me on edge. But the best part wasnt the horror for me, it was the story. The story behind this game is abosolutely amazing. My favorite detail added to this game is that very very early on in the game do you get the mission to escape. In most classic horror games the player usually navigates the place with an objective to reach something or do something, but in Outlast, the second the player realizes the place is dangerous, he decides to leave. I think that the camcorder was also well setup and a great addition to the horror aspect. Having to manage the enemies while trying to find limited batteries so that you can see where your going in an almost completely dark asylum is nerve wracking. Besides that though, the entire game is just a great story horror game that keeps you reeled in to whats going on and constantly poses new threats. It isnt a long game, I beat it in only 10 hours, but its one of the great horror games that set the stage down the line.

I’m a bit late to this game, but it was really fun and really scary too. The story was actually good, but one thing I have to say… this game is seriously messed up in the best way possible.

I thought the first game was already pretty disturbing until I started playing the second one. I haven’t finished it yet, but it’s way more messed up than I expected. Somehow, though, that makes it even better. It adds so much to the horror and makes the experience more intense and memorable.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

I started playing Outlast thinking it would just be a creepy horror game to mess around with for a bit… and it turned into one of the most intense games I’ve played in a long time.

The whole atmosphere is just unsettling in the best way. Walking through that asylum with nothing but a camcorder and night vision makes everything feel way more real than it should. There were definitely a few moments where I caught myself literally holding my breath while hiding in a locker hoping whatever was chasing me would just walk past.

I also didn’t expect the story to pull me in as much as it did. It’s dark, weird, and the deeper you get into it the more you want to keep playing just to figure out what’s actually going on.

If you like horror games that rely more on tension and atmosphere instead of just cheap jump scares, this one absolutely delivers. Just make sure you’re ready to be a little stressed the entire time you’re playing.

219223698.review:
I just finished playing Outlast for the second or third time, and honestly, I loved it.

The story is incredibly deep, disturbing, and packed with rich backstory and theories that make you think long after the credits roll. For a game made by a small team with a limited budget, everything comes together shockingly well. The movement feels smooth, the mechanics work great, the voice acting is solid, and the atmosphere is absolutely relentless. It constantly keeps you on edge, never letting you feel safe, and everything actually makes sense when you look at the bigger picture.

The Whistleblower DLC is amazing as well. It adds important context and helps you truly understand what was really happening behind the scenes, making the overall story even stronger.

The achievements are fairly easy to obtain, except for the Insane runs. Those were a bit too much for me. I gave up on the Lunatic achievement after eight attempts, and I’ll admit defeat there.

Overall, this is an outstanding horror game that delivers on atmosphere, storytelling, and tension. Highly recommended.

Outlast is pure survival horror done right. From the moment you step inside Mount Massive Asylum, the atmosphere is absolutely suffocating—in the best way possible. The game wastes no time throwing you into terrifying situations where your only options are to run, hide, or pray you’re not found.

The lack of combat makes every encounter intense. You’re completely vulnerable, and that vulnerability is what makes the experience so unforgettable. The night-vision camera mechanic adds a brilliant layer of tension, especially when your batteries start running low at the worst possible time.

The sound design, environments, and pacing all come together to create nonstop dread. Every hallway feels dangerous, and every sound makes you second-guess your next move.

If you’re looking for a horror game that truly commits to fear and keeps your heart racing from start to finish, Outlast is a must-play.

218817357.review:
The best story-driven game I have ever played.

I’m not exaggerating when I say that Outlast is not just a horror game — it’s an experience that stays with you long after you close it.

From the moment you step inside Mount Massive Asylum, the atmosphere is suffocating. Every sound makes you hold your breath. The fact that you can’t fight back — that your only options are to run or hide — makes everything ten times more intense. You’re not a hero. You’re prey.

The story is incredibly well crafted. It doesn’t spoon-feed you answers. You uncover the truth through documents, recordings, and environmental details, and that makes it even more powerful. The world feels disturbingly real.

This game doesn’t rely only on cheap jumpscares. It breaks you psychologically. It makes you feel small, vulnerable, and completely powerless — exactly what true horror should do.

For me, this is the best story-driven game I’ve played so far.

10/10. A masterpiece of horror.

218416832.review:
Outlast advanced the horror genre by emphasizing highly realistic environments and visuals with a completely non-violent player character. Players unveil a compelling story by navigating the asylum. At first, it is gut-wrenchingly scary, but in a short time you'll become accustomed to the general pattern of the game in a way that removed that fear for me. First of all, the game is almost entirely linear. At most, you might have to go separate ways to turn off two switches, then come back to the main switch to proceed with the game. Most "puzzles" are like that. The enemy AI becomes similarly predictable, with most enemy challenges requiring you to hide while they pass you so you can go the other way, or at worst, hopping over a table while they chase you so that you can juke them out. You get used to understanding if you're in an exploration part of the game, a sneaky part of the game, or a chase sequence, and once you know what the expectation is it becomes much easier to identify what you need to do to succeed.

I enjoyed myself throughout, but the core appeal of powerlessness (common in the genre these days), was a major detriment for me. I just kept thinking it wouldn't have gotten so bad if the player characters would just fight back. The linear environment felt like a missed opportunity. The asylum is super intriguing, and I would have loved to explore it more diegetically, but ultimately every door is locked except the one you need to go through.

I'm giving this a positive review because it was an enjoyable experience and a milestone in the genre, so anyone looking to expand their horror history knowledge should definitely pick it up, but like most horror games I stopped feeling scared once I figured out how the rails worked. Then it was just a movie I had to press buttons for. But, a good movie.

It throws you into many scenarios that make you feel helpless and weak which makes the overall experience terrifying. There's a ton of different enemies and chuds rushing you constantly so its nonstop thriller. If you like horror or are looking to get into one, give this a try.

217812900.review:
Outlast taught me an important life lesson: running is always better than fighting.

You don’t shoot. You don’t fight. You don’t survive with skill. You survive by screaming internally, hiding in lockers, and praying the batteries don’t die at the worst possible moment (they always do).

The atmosphere is absolutely brutal. The sound design alone is enough to raise your heart rate, and the darkness feels genuinely threatening. Every hallway feels unsafe, and every door could be a terrible mistake.

Is it fun? Yes. Is it stressful? Extremely. Did I keep playing even though I was scared? Unfortunately, yes.

The story is disturbing, the pacing is intense, and the game never lets you feel comfortable — not even for a second. And that’s exactly why it works.

If you enjoy horror games that replace combat with pure panic and make you question why you started playing at night, Outlast is a must-play.

Highly recommended — just don’t forget extra batteries (and courage).

Fantastic game. Drenched in atmosphere and intrigue. 

The chase sequences always remain tense despite how far through the game you are. It varies the sequences with different enemies, areas and objectives to keep you on your toes. Outlast does the 'Amensia' thing of stripping any form of defence away from you, but it's a lot more fast-paced and engaging. There's also some light platforming that usually involves clearing a gap or jumping up a ledge while under pressure. 

There aren't any puzzles as such, but you often have to seek out resources for repairs, or keys to open doors while being stalked by some kind of ungodly specimen. 

The story and writing are great. There's a lot of density and weight behind the asylum that you traverse. [spoiler]References to CIA secret operations, Nazi occultism and patient experimentation are highlights. [/spoiler] 

This is probably legit one of the best horror games ever made. At the time of writing this review the game is over 10 years old, and having just played it I can confidently say that it holds up amazing well. No brainer if its on sale as well, it goes for peanuts.

217310432.review:
Having 224 hours on record and 100% achievements on this game, I think I can provide a useful review. This is a great game and excels at what it was made for. That said, it is not for everybody. Thus, I shall be listing what to and what not to expect from it.

What to expect:
- Survival horror: there is no fighting (on your part), running and hiding is the aim of the game.
- Jump scares: yes, it's a survival HORROR for a reason. Keep a spare pair of underwear available when you are playing this game.
- Explicit and dark: violence, gore, vulgar language, some nudity and sexually related incidents all play a part in creating the dark, horrifying atmosphere of Outlast.
- Interesting storyline and background information: The storyline has a bit of a twist in the tale, things aren't exactly as you expected them to be. Also, with all the notes and documents one collects in this game, one gets a decent amount of background information which helps with the atmosphere of the game, develops the plot (and player understanding thereof) and gives the player insight as to the nature of the silent protagonist, as well as some of the antagonists you'll be facing.

What not to expect:
- Hours of replay value: your first playthrough will probably be about 5-6 hours and the DLC about 1-2 hours. As the game progression is always the same, there is little reason to play it again (with the exception of wanting to get achievements).
- Competitiveness: There is no form of multiplayer or leaderboards of any kind.
- Something suitable for children (you should have picked that up by now)
- Sunshine and roses
- Clean underwear
- Being able to sleep at night (slightly exaggerated)

I absolutely loved this game and highly recommend it to anyone that is a fan of the horror genre, whether it be movies or games. I hope the information listed will help you in your decision of whether or not to purchase this game.

Happy Gaming!

216984573.review:
Outlast is one of the standout entries of 2010s horror gaming. Drawing clear inspiration from titles like Amnesia: The Dark Descent, it embraces survival horror without weapons, focusing instead on vulnerability, atmosphere, and evasion. While it largely follows that established formula, it somewhat innovates in its storytelling mechanics.

Your primary tool is a handheld camera equipped with night vision, which serves both as your means of navigation and as a narrative device. Batteries, scattered throughout the environment, are required to keep it functioning, turning light itself into a limited resource while reinforcing the game’s documentary framing as you record the horrors of the asylum.

I look back fondly on this game. If you enjoy survival horror, and especially if you enjoy the Amnesia series, you will likely enjoy Outlast.

216981734.review:
[h3][b]Outlast turns curiosity into fear[/b][/h3]

Outlast is a first-person survival horror game where, as a journalist, you break into an asylum with only a video camera whose night vision drains batteries. Its biggest strength is the atmosphere. Lighting, sound, and pacing sustain the tension, making every step forward and every new discovery feel as unsettling as it is addictive.

In my case, it kept me invested in understanding what was happening all the way to the end, and it genuinely scared me in a few scenes. The big BUT is that the hide-and-seek gameplay feels weak, and the NPC AI can be clumsy, making those sections more annoying than intense. I’m not sure if it was because I played it on Steam Deck, but there were times it literally gave me a headache. Still, if you’re looking for psychological horror and an oppressive experience, Outlast is a great game you should try.

216756849.review:
Outlast (2013) is the father of modern horror.

In today's world filled with horror games, finding something truly interesting and actually playable can be challenging (and I'm not just talking about horror games, of course). Outlast 2013 is a perfect example of storytelling and scriptwriting that drags you into terror from the very beginning, when you're faced with enormous blocks of a psychiatric facility filled with people whose minds barely exist in reality. You might think this is normal for patients in such a place, but as you go deeper into the storyline and explore the darkest corners of the clinic, you discover there are much darker reasons behind their conditions.

From a metaphorical point of view, the game shows life's constant struggle—where you're always trying to overcome problems but can't always fight back. Sometimes you can only run, hide, and desperately search for a way out. But will you have a chance to escape? If you do, will it be the right path for you? Or is your destiny already sealed?

Personally, Outlast is one of my favorite games of all time. Red Barrels created a true monster of fear and delivered it in the best possible way. Great storyline, great graphics, great characters. People have been trying for years to solve the game's mysteries and theories, and nobody ever gets tired of its existence.

So my recommendation is based on everything I've just written: it's your choice whether to play or not, and your choice whether to find your way out of this nightmare—or not.

216710896.review:
=========== Ending ==========
I played the game twice. Father Martin tasks you with recording verifiable evidence of the wrong doings of the Murkoff Asylum and every path you were supposed to take were planned by him and the patients. Actually all of the patients including the people that try to k*** you are part of it, they are placed there to "keep you going", except the obese person who thinks you're the Walrider. At the end Father Martin finally gives you the key to go leave the asylum, BUT the elevator was overrode by Dr. Wernicke who sends you to the secret hidden floor and gives you the task of pulling the life support on Billy Hope because he's the host of the Walrider, the invisible nanomachines. You finish the task however you coincidentally become the new host after Billy goes into you and the requirement for being the host was "close proximity to death" or "experiencing horrors". You think you're finally free and... that's as far as I'm gonna go.
.
Dr. Wernicke and everyone else is a victim. They are forced to do what they do, for the exorbitant amount of $$$ the top executives think they will make after it's finished. Which unfortunately all of them are k***.
=========== Positive Review ===========
After playing both the base game and the DLC Whistleblower, I have come to the understanding that the reason the mc is silent with no dialogue is because it symbolically represents you as the player. You're venturing into a facility that silences people through involuntary psychiatric hospitalization i.e. claiming you're mentally ill for attempting to leak to the world that the hospital does experiments on normal people.
.
With a government that doesn't uphold the law but instead sides with the facility to silence YOU the player and anyone else that may leak what happened.
=========== Critical Review ============
It suffers from a lack of human interaction, semi-realism, and raised stakes. You can't really feel the moment when you're not there. I'm making this part based on what game should be, not with moral values.
.
There's not enough human speech and interaction.
The music and certain background sound effects sometimes break realism.
Recording with the camera has little value or of importance. - Little stakes involved
It has little contrast between what is dark and what isn't. i.e. theres too much blood
.
My only favorite part about this game was the "doctor" guy who made feel like I was actually in the game experiencing it.
.
It would be great if the mc had more reactions or dialogues.
I grew accustomed to seeing blood.
There's too little jump scares
There's too little special cutscenes i.e. someone dragging a person away
There's too few villains
There's too few villains with a variety of personality with a map that accompanies them
There's too little importance or emphasis placed on achieving a goal - turning on a power generator
There's little punishments for not having recognized a map layout before going back to it
There's little emotional impact - i.e. hands trembling while inserting a key into a door during a chase
Optional side effect 1: Uploading the video files to the web before having escaped
Optional side effect 2: Using the Whistleblowers mc's computer skills to hack into the facility to turn off firewall and steal gigabytes of data, enable wifi for himself before "attempting" to upload all data and video files into the web.
There's little obstacles, camera gets damaged(broken/wet) or stolen from you and you have to chase down the perpetrator or data gets corrupted
.
Summary: The events feel scripted.

216590101.review:
⭐ Recommended – Recomendado (ENG / ES)

ENG:

Game:

Outlast is a first-person horror game focused on survival, exploration, and atmosphere. You play as a journalist trapped inside an abandoned asylum, with no weapons and very limited resources. The game relies heavily on sound design, environmental storytelling, and tension rather than constant action.

Personal Opinion:

This is easily one of the best suspense and horror games out there, and it was absolutely worth playing. The game itself isn’t scary in a traditional way — it’s the atmosphere that gets you. The sounds, breathing, footsteps, and ambient noise do most of the work. Even knowing that there are jumpscares, the tension never really goes away, and you still get caught off guard from time to time.

It’s one of those games where you’re constantly on edge, not because something is always happening, but because you’re waiting for something to happen — and that’s what makes it so good.

Technical Stuff:

I ran into a weird issue where, if I paused the game and used the Windows key to go to the desktop, the game wouldn’t properly reopen. I could move the mouse and open apps, but I couldn’t interact with anything or close the game. It didn’t happen all the time, but when it did, it was pretty annoying.

Completion Notes:

I recommend playing the game on Normal difficulty first, taking your time, and collecting all the collectible while going at your own pace. That way, you really get to enjoy the horror and atmosphere.

When you go for Insane mode, the experience changes completely. Once you start speedrunning and memorizing routes, the game honestly isn’t that scary anymore — it becomes more about execution than fear. For Insane mode, I highly recommend pausing after every major chase, taking a moment to breathe, grabbing a drink, and relaxing before continuing. It helps more than you’d think.

ES:

Juego:

Outlast es un juego de terror en primera persona centrado en la supervivencia, la exploración y la atmósfera. Juegas como un periodista atrapado dentro de un manicomio abandonado, sin armas y con recursos muy limitados. El juego depende en gran medida del diseño de sonido, la narrativa ambiental y la tensión, más que de la acción constante.

Opinión Personal:

Fácilmente es uno de los mejores juegos de suspenso y terror que existen, y definitivamente valió la pena jugarlo. El juego en sí no da miedo de una forma tradicional; es la atmósfera la que realmente te atrapa. Los sonidos, la respiración, los pasos y el ruido ambiental hacen casi todo el trabajo. Incluso sabiendo que hay jumpscares, la tensión nunca desaparece del todo, y aun así logra tomarte por sorpresa de vez en cuando.

Es uno de esos juegos en los que estás constantemente en alerta, no porque siempre esté pasando algo, sino porque estás esperando a que pase — y eso es lo que lo hace tan bueno.

Aspectos Técnicos:

Me encontré con un problema extraño en el que, si pausaba el juego y usaba la tecla de Windows para ir al escritorio, el juego no se reabría correctamente. Podía mover el mouse y abrir otras aplicaciones, pero no podía interactuar con nada ni cerrar el juego. No pasaba todo el tiempo, pero cuando ocurría, resultaba bastante molesto.

Notas de Completado:

Recomiendo jugar primero en dificultad Normal, tomándote tu tiempo y recogiendo todos los documentos mientras avanzas a tu propio ritmo. De esa forma puedes disfrutar realmente del terror y la atmósfera.

Cuando vas por el modo Insane, la experiencia cambia por completo. Una vez que empiezas a speedrunear y memorizar las rutas, el juego sinceramente deja de dar tanto miedo — se vuelve más una cuestión de ejecución que de terror. Para el modo Insane, recomiendo mucho pausar después de cada persecución importante, tomarte un momento para respirar, agarrar algo de beber y relajarte antes de continuar. Ayuda más de lo que parece.

216590023.review:
This game is one of my favorites of all time, and has been for over a decade. 
The whole series makes a really interesting commentary on historical atrocities, such as MKUltra, Nazi experimentation (and the result: Operation Paperclip); Outlast II and Trials reference the Tuskegee Syphilis experiment. Ultimately, from patient lines (the ones that talk about how they're people and didn't choose this), historical references, and the overall tragedy that you see when you get past the splatterpunk exterior, the message I got from it is that when we marginalize people, we leave them vulnerable to exploitation. The patients may be the immediate physical threats (and tbh it's only some of them; the rest are terrified and mutilated and left to rot), but it's Murkoff that's the real enemy. I love media that SAYS something. They did a fantastic job of that here.

Reader: go look up the comics if you haven't already! They're free on the devs' website!

I like that you have to dig into the notes and documents to get a sense of the scope of what happened. I love feeling like a detective. Gameplay is fun, the characters are delightful, the voice acting is great, the game has a sense of humor, and overall I love it dearly. 

Thank you, Red Barrels, for making such great games <3

216366129.review:
Look, it’s been over a decade, and Outlast still feels like a fever dream you can’t wake up from. If you’re looking for a reason to never sleep again, this game is basically a one-way ticket to cardiac arrest.

Seriously, how does a game from 2013 still look this good? The atmosphere doesn't just set the mood. It suffocates you.

Seeing the world through that camcorder lens adds this voyeuristic, found footage layer that makes everything feel too real. And when you flick on that green glow and see a pair of eyes reflecting back at you from the end of a pitch-black hallway? That’s peak horror aesthetic. It’s gritty, it’s industrial, and it’s dripping with dread.

The enemies here aren't just scary. They’re traumatic. You’ve got Chris Walker hunting you like a relentless juggernaut and Dr. Trager giving you the worst check-up of your life. The DLC somehow turns the volume up to eleven. Frank Manera and Eddie Gluskin are absolute nightmare fuel. Gluskin's wedding preparations are some of the most genuinely disturbing scenes ever put in a game. Period.

What makes it work is that this isn't a power fantasy. You aren't a hero. You’re a witness. There is no fight button. You are completely helpless. Your entire existence boils down to the sound of your own frantic breathing as you scramble under a bed or dive into a locker. That vulnerability is what makes the terror stick.

The best part? It doesn’t drag. Outlast and Whistleblower are lean, mean, horror machines. They hit you with high-octane trauma and then get out before the gimmick gets old. It’s a tight, focused descent into madness that respects your time while ruining your nerves.

Just put on some headphones, turn off the lights, and prepare to regret every life choice that led you to Mount Massive.

216177403.review:
[h1][b]Outlast[/b][/h1]
[b]Genre:[/b] Horror / Survival, Psychological
[b]Perspective:[/b] 1st Person
[b]My Rating:[/b]⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

💙Summary (TLDR):
[i]I absolutely love this horror game with its premise of being absolutely defenseless in the ''abandoned" asylum trying to survive as the only sane person left by hiding and using a nightvision camera with just limited access to batteries. The story is actually great and I love both Miles and Waylon as our protagonists. I surely do recommend it however I am quite dulled towards dark/repulsive themes in horror games so maybe if you don’t have strong nerves or have issues with gore or abusive themes you should consider if you can get through this. There are some moments that are highly questionable if you have light nerves![/i]

[h3][b]The Positives[/b][/h3]
[list][*]The game’s premise and atmosphere is amazing. The almost abandoned and creepy asylum that’s falling apart might be a cliché setting within horror but it’s done in a way here that’s realistic and believable! Both the silence and the specific situations with musical tunes are fitting and add to the dreadful atmosphere and feeling of the place.
[*]The game might first appear to not really have a story but it is told through the many notes you can find and photos you need to shoot at specific events to unlock commentary on the reporter’s and whistleblower’s notepads. It’s a really tragic story actually. Especially the twist at the end is done nicely, the extra situation at the DLC’s end wraps it up wonderfully!
[*]The main mechanic of the game is that you are utterly defenseless with no weapons or any other means to fight back against the various patients. What you have is a camera with nightvision but said function uses up batteries which are scarce to find; it adds another mechanic of learning and guessing where you really need the camera’s light or where it’s safe finding your way through the darkness on your own. Both mechanics cause an uneasy feeling when playing this game for the first time but they also add a fun layer of learning and memorizing the game’s events and areas for other playthroughs to no longer depend on the camera’s nightvision at all.
[*]The game does not hold back with its shockvalue. Many things presented (auditory and visually) are disgusting, repulsive, perverse and for people with light nerves could be even a bit nauseating. Even if the graphics might be a bit dated for nowadays standards, I think it all still holds up fine and presents a neat horror experience, especially with headphones.
[*]The “bosses” are all only there for short moments but they make these moments memorable. Trager is both terrifying but also a morbid jester, Chris is a scary and powerful menace but turns into a tragic character once you learn why he is the way he is.
[*]But the most memorable “boss” of them all to me is still Eddie Gluskin, who we meet in the DLC. He is a fascinating character that I believe manages to make everyone feel uncomfortable and miserable - the game both explicitly shows the insane amount of horror he inflicts to men he captures and gives us enough hints about what women would have to endure would he be able to find one so neither male nor female gamers are spared the discomfort - his voiceactor also did an insanely good job portraying him, especially when he starts to sing his signature song!
[/list]
[h3][b]The “Not So Goods”[/b][/h3]
[list][*]I will never be able to play this game again for the first time. No kidding, I love this game and it’s still one of my favorite horror games to this day!
[/list]

215573998.review:
I started off by playing the Whistle blower dlc. and I have to say, Outlast is nothing like I'd expected. 
Most horror games these days rely on overused mechanics, or a very tired zombie hoards trope. Dont get me wrong, I played Left for Dead to death, but I've always hoped for more originality. Something different, something truly scary that gets stuck in your mind. 

Outlast does this extremely well. 
Thanks to the recent Steam winter sale, I was able to snag the whole franchise plus dlcs for about the price of a super size meal. Well. Worth. It.   Even at full price this game is worth it. 

Replayable, especially for serious challenge seekers. I struggled on the lowest difficulty - and loved doing it. 

Outlast is a game that has it's fair share of jumpscares, juicy gore, and all of the things you'd normally think of in the horror genre, BUT what's special about it is when it uses your own senses against you. You are forced not only to master key strokes, timing, and clicks, but your natural instincts to run and hide.   It feels like being in a lucid nightmare at times when you want to run or scream - but you can't.   Is there something waiting around the corner? Is the only danger bodily harm or something...worse than just pain. Humiliation. Vulnerability. Powerlessness.   The things that true nightmares are made of.  Superbly done. 

There are parts of the story and scenes that are... disgustingly adult. I don't want to spoil it, but some of this is absolutely - deeply - disturbing. I didn't expect that aspect, ALSO it is not stream friendly (later versions such as trials let you block out any nudity for example so it can be streamed safely.)

All in all - if you "like" horror, even really messed up horror. This will not disappoint you.     Don't even worry about it being a little dated here in 2026 - it's still honestly a masterpiece.

215401364.review:
Outlast strips you of control and never gives it back. You enter Mount Massive with nothing but a camcorder, and the game immediately makes it clear you’re not meant to survive. There’s no fighting — only running, hiding, and hoping you don’t get found.

The worst moments are when even the camcorder fails you and you’re forced through near-total darkness, relying on sound and instinct while something could be right beside you. Losing your only sense of safety feels deliberate and cruel.

The game doesn’t just scare you — it takes things from you, including parts of your body, to remind you how fragile you are. You hide in lockers and under beds while broken people pace inches away, less monsters than remnants of something deeply wrong.

Outlast slowly twists what you think the real horror is, replacing it with something far more disturbing. The ending is brutal, disorienting, and perfect.

If you love helpless, psychological horror that lingers, Outlast delivers.

215187705.review: Wow! Spectacular. Uncomfortable. Thrilling.  I held off playing this one for years, chalk it up to anxiety and fear but now I am pleased to have played it.  I will have to agree with the praise and accolades on OUTLAST:  It is a perfectly paced horror experience.  I played on Normal and finished the game basically in one sitting.  This is a testament to the design and pacing.  Outlast carries that great tradition of survival/stealth horror imbuing you with a sense of dread as well as purpose, exhaustion coupled with drive, tension and then relief.  There are so many really great scares and set ups within an atmosphere that is deep and brooding.  The mechanics are seamlessly integrated and it's really fun (and scary) to be chased around.  The sound and visuals are thick and cling to you, giving a claustrophobic vibe.  Glad I got to this one finally!  If you enjoy the genre and other titles like Alien: Isolation or maybe the Amnesia series and somehow (like myself until recently) had not dabbled in the madness you should check this one out!   I couldn't recommend it more.

214994408.review:
I have somehow played [b][i]three[/i][/b] separate games where I am chased by naked men who want to eat me: Outlast, The Forest, and Rust. While I am entirely unsure what Freud would comment about my psychological state, I can say that Outlast is definitely the best of the bunch.

It's dark, gruesome, and downright terrifying without relying on ultra-cheap jumpscares. It takes the whole found footage phenomenon and refines it into the 24-karat gold standard that so many other horror games aspire, and usually fail, to be. The Whistleblower DLC cranks up the gruesome factor to 11 and is absolutely [b][i]mandatory[/i][/b]. One of the best horror games of the 2010s.
