214850743.review:
While it made for a fun experience and great ambience, it was not without issue and ultimately flawed in its character treatment and failure in storytelling.

It is a work of undeniable technical proficiency in atmosphere, yet it is ultimately undone by a juvenile approach to characterization and a narrative trajectory that falls apart. The ambient hum of the facility, the wet slap of bare feet on tile, and the hyper-ventilating breathing of the protagonist create a soundscape of vulnerability. The asylum feels lived-in capturing the rotting institutional decay.

The game relies heavily on mutilation and sexual violence to elicit a reaction, mistaking transgressive imagery for psychological depth. The antagonists, such as Chris Walker or Richard Trager, are memorable not for their motivations or their tragedy, but simply for their gross-out factor. They are monsters in a slasher film, devoid of the humanity that makes horror truly disturbing. And their climactic showdowns reduced to throwing them away. In retrospect, the most fearsome character proved to be the one generic guy wandering around the generator in the basement all alone.

[¶ Spoilers] What becomes of Walker? He gets pulled into a vent by a mysterious spoopy ghost. And Trager, in all his malice drags you into the depths of his ward to cut two fingers off, because that's the scary thing to do? After you free yourself from his dastardly plan of… leaving you in a bathroom, you run around his ward looking for fuses. And then he spots you, chases you and the entire section turns into a game of tag until you notice the vent above a desk. Well, he gets smushed by an elevator. That is in effect the entirety of levels, be it Trager, Walker, the Twins etc. The Twins, another set of nemeses, are hellbent on killing you from first encounter. The climax of their involvement is a showdown in the theater. At least that’s how it’s presented. But instead you escape, and they are literally down the hall holding the chapel doors open for you because the priest told them so. That’s the last you see of them. And the priest, made out to be a vital element of revealing the depraved secrets of Mount Massive, just kills himself. His denouement is here's the elevator key *dies*.

If the characters weren’t wasted enough, the plot of Outlast suffers from a severe identity crisis. It begins as a grounded albeit extreme exploration of institutional abuse and unethical psychiatric experimentation. However, the third act pivot introducing Walrider abandons the gritty realism of the asylum for pseudo-scientific mysticism that feels entirely unearned. The narrative resolution here renders the player's struggle somewhat performative. The shift from "survival horror" to "nanotech sci-fi" breaks the suspension of disbelief. The ending is abrupt, and nihilistic without purpose, and leaves the player with a sense of "That’s it??" rather than a lingering existential dread.

If you are looking for a horror narrative that respects its characters or provides a satisfying and cohesive conclusion, you will find the asylum structurally sound but intellectually vacant.

214309987.review: Played through both Outlast and Whistleblower back to back and combined this game  is one of the best and most truly horrifying games I've ever played. The worldbuilding is mysterious and leaves just the right amount of bread crumbs for you to keep wanting more. The pacing is well done, with many high-intensity chases and hiding sequences, but still intervals where you can take in the sheer depravity, brokenness, and even sorrow of the world around you. The puzzles are relatively straightforward but a healthy level of challenging to accomplish. The soundtrack is impeccable, and the imagery of some areas will be forever engrained into my memory, some beautiful and some scarring. The characters are all well written and bear unique identity making them complex enemies (or possibly friends) to face. All in all an amazing horror experience that left me wanting to redo it all again. Would recommend getting Whistleblower for further context to the story and a more enriching experience.

A very light form of Survival Horror where conserving resources and backtracking for key items is substituted for grabbing batteries to power your night vision camera and navigating through a mostly linear, but very large building. 

A majority of the appeal comes from the "Haunted House romp" vibes as you progress through a disgusting, torn environment being chased down by a variety of psychopaths. The soundtrack stood out quite a bit and a few of the characters are pretty memorable. It's not the most intense or unique horror game but it wasn't a bad time in the slightest.

Although, you may want to go into the game's .ini file to change some settings like removing film grain (which appears even without looking through your camera, for whatever reason) or the 60FPS cap.

213605138.review:
While I think it's a great game, it hasn't aged well at all; This game is from 2013 and you can tell.
Great atmosphere and sound design, good environments and characters, great story overall.
However, the over-reliance on cheap jumpscares really hurts the game overall, but that's a product of it being from 2013, the DLC does a way better job at being legitimately scary. The terrifying atmosphere and sound design is undermined by the fact that I have to turn my volume down in order to not get my eardrums blown out by the constant jumpscares, it's incredibly annoying. 

The level design is actually really bad, I had to look up walkthroughs multiple times because the game does a horrible job at conditioning you to do certain things that you need to do in order to progress. (Such as looking up, jumping on things, or squeezing between things)
Additionally, there are way too many dead-ends that you can get stuck in thinking that you're missing something, it's a huge problem that the game has and makes a first blind playthrough a pain.

Luckily, the sequel fixed literally all of these problems so the developers clearly understand the assignment and are amazing and you should give them your money. This game is sort of a dud but it's still worth playing.

213599642.review: i liked it! it was scary got me screaming lol

213592346.review:
[h1]Freaking awesome horror game btw[/h1]

Outlast is a horror game where you play as a journalist who is going to investigate an asylum after you get a tip from an anonymous source about suspicous activity going on there. This game has pretty fun section by section gameplay, an interesting story and overall does great with it's horror elements. 
I'll say, usually I'm not the biggest fan of horror games, mostly because I'm not an easily scared person (oh look at me I'm so tough!!), but Outlast actually looked like it could be pretty damn scary! And to be honest, my expectations were met pretty freaking well!!! There were genuinely surprising jumpscares at some points that caught me off guard and they never felt cheaply placed throughout the game imo, like there was no point where I felt like the game was overusing them, which was nice.
I have nothing bad to say about the gameplay in general, most of what you have to do is trying to conserve your battery so you can use night vision and whatnot! But there is one very small thing to nitpick on, which is the difficulty settings, as it doesn't really change much unless you are playing on the highest difficulty (which has permadeath), the only real change is how many batteries you get and can carry, which does not change the gameplay much. Unless you are constantly using your night vision and burning through batteries.
Story wise, I'll try not to spoil much, but the way you learn about the story is through documents that you find, which give you more context and backstory for some characters and the overall main plot going on in the game, which is interesting to read through. 
The ONLY gameplay section that I felt that was disconnected from the rest of the game in a way that didn't feel like it "fit" was the final chapter, while fine from a story perspective, it broke my immersion and wasn't nearly as scary as the rest of the game. Though I wouldn't say it was bad, just very disconnected to me in my opinion!
My personal favorite part of the game was actually the DLC content, in which you play as the anonymous informant that actually leads the main character into the asylum in the first place. Despite how short it was in comparison to the main game, the whole DLC was packed full of interesting enemies, the same thrilling scenery from the main game and among other things expanded the story for the game AND the ending in a way that felt really cool.
All in all, Outlast has a good fear factor, good gameplay, an interesting story, a fantastic DLC and doesn't have any major issues that make the game worse to play! Definetly worth checking out if you are a fan of awesome and fun horror games!

213443658.review:
Outlast is one of the most intense horror games I’ve ever played. The atmosphere is terrifying from start to finish, with dark environments, disturbing enemies, and constant tension that never really lets you relax. The fact that you can’t fight back and must rely on hiding and running makes every encounter feel genuinely scary.

The story is intriguing and slowly revealed through documents and exploration, which adds to the immersion. The sound design is excellent and plays a huge role in building fear, especially when you’re alone in the dark with only a night-vision camera.

Overall, Outlast is a must-play for horror fans. It’s not very long, but the experience is unforgettable and absolutely worth it.

213323671.review:
Great horror game, although it did frustrate me when I would hide behind a line of sight blocker and the AI would still find me; unless it was one of the designated "HIDE HERE" items, being a desk, locker, or bed. I'm used to stealth games working on a Line of Sight system rather than having a circle around the enemy that determines whether or not they see you. There was a section near the end of the game where I hid behind a door, thinking I could just walk past the main antagonist, Chris Walker, while his back was to me and slip by to search for the exit. Chris walked into the room and immediately locked onto me behind the door despite his head never turning to face me. That took my immersion out. Otherwise, I had a lot of fun with the game! Rather short experience, but I would play it again.

Edit: After playing through the Whistleblower DLC and taking more time to experiment with the stealth mechanics, my opinion on the game is much fonder. The stealth is finnicky to figure out what makes an enemy catch you. Sprinting is an instant catch, and standing negates whatever hiding spot you're hiding behind. With this in mind, I got through the game with a much more enjoyable session. The game and DLC are short, but the story is great.

213077832.review:
Outlast has been one of my favourite horror games ever since it first released in 2013, and it still holds up incredibly well today. It’s a true classic in the horror genre—simple, terrifying, and made exactly the way a horror game should be.

The atmosphere is intense from start to finish, with amazing sound design and environments that keep you on edge the entire way through. The story is disturbing in all the right ways, and the game never stops delivering those memorable, heart-pounding moments.

Whether you’re revisiting it or playing for the first time, Outlast remains a must-play for any horror fan. A timeless classic that set the bar for modern horror games.

Outlast is hands-down one of the best pure horror experiences I’ve played. The atmosphere is tense from the very start, and the game never really gives you a moment to breathe. The jump scares are incredibly well-timed, the sound design keeps you on edge, and the story pulls you deeper into the madness of Mount Massive Asylum.

I streamed my entire play-through, and it took me about 7 hours to finish. Not once did it feel slow or dragged out, every section had me fully locked in. The game does an amazing job at making you feel helpless, which makes the scares hit even harder. If you’re looking for a terrifying, adrenaline-pumping horror game, Outlast absolutely delivers.

Highly recommend to anyone who loves real tension, dark atmosphere, and unforgettable scares.

209568729.review:
This game is genuinely ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ terrifying. Not just scary—terrifying. You have no weapons, no way to fight back, just a camera and your ability to run and hide. The helplessness is what gets you.
The night vision mechanic creates insane tension. When that battery starts dying and you're trying to navigate in the dark while something's chasing you—your heart will be pounding. The sound design is incredible and will mess with your head.
Fair warning: this isn't for everyone. It's relentless, disturbing, and the kind of horror that actually gets under your skin. I had to take breaks because the tension was exhausting. The story goes to some really dark places.
Whistleblower DLC is even more intense than the base game and adds crucial story elements. If you can handle the main game, definitely play it—but it's somehow even more ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up.
If you have a weak heart or get anxious easily, maybe skip this one. But if you want a horror game that actually horrifies you? This is it.
Recommended for serious horror fans who can handle extreme content.

209466717.review:
Outlast and its Whistleblower DLC together make one of the most intense horror experiences I’ve played. The main game throws you into Mount Massive Asylum with nothing but a camera, and the atmosphere stays tense from start to finish. The sense of being hunted and having to hide instead of fight works perfectly and keeps your nerves tight the whole way through.

Whistleblower ties the whole story together really well. It adds context to what actually happened at the asylum and shows events from a different perspective. Even though it’s shorter, the pacing feels tighter, and the tension doesn’t let up for a second.

Both parts share the same strong points: great sound design, a constantly eerie atmosphere, and a story that stays engaging without needing huge cutscenes. Some chase moments repeat a bit, but overall the experience is gripping and memorable.

Rating: 9/10 — Best played together for the full story.

208870501.review:
A very atmospheric story about a journalist that went into hell and back to unveil a horrigic mysteries of evil corporation!

Outlast is a product of its time, an interesting horror story with a character, who cant even fight back the escaped horrors of asylum. Very atmospheric, very fun in general.

From the good sides of the game:
- A very atmospheric horror story, with a lot of details (even through mostly in the notes), very dark and spooky old Mount Massive Asylum building owned by a greedy company. All of that also being pretty gorey and bloody in general.
- Dynamic gameplay that is good for such a horro story: run, hide, or die and try again!
- Good work with sound design, you would probably want a headphones to be able to hear your surrounding properly
- Game isnt too big and long, so it doesnt get too tiresome

- Gameplay might be dynamic, but it can get repetative over time and tiresome, luckily the game isnt that big
- Unskippable cutscenes. Cutscenes here is a part of gameplay, so they cannot be skipped. Which isnt an issue, unless you do a Nightmare run that only has 1 life and if u die, you have to watch it all over again. Every, damn, time.

Overall Outlast is a very cool game for back in the day and still holds good. I sure enjoyed playing it, even through nightmares runs were a bit hard at some points.
8/10 Miles Upshir fingers (get it? Because Tragger cut off 2 fingers? No? Don't mind me, I will find the door...)

208599620.review: Outlast is one of the most intense horror experiences I’ve ever had in a game. You play as an investigative journalist armed with nothing but a camcorder, sneaking through the dark, rotting halls of Mount Massive Asylum. There are no weapons, no fighting back — just running, hiding, and praying whatever’s chasing you loses interest. The atmosphere is phenomenal — flickering lights, distant screams, and the constant buzz of your night vision make every step feel dangerous. The sound design deserves special praise; every creak or breath in the dark puts you on edge. The story, told through documents and environmental details, builds a disturbing and believable world full of twisted experiments and religious madness. The game isn’t perfect — it’s fairly linear, and the chase sequences can feel trial-and-error at times. But if you’re looking for raw, nerve-shredding terror, Outlast absolutely delivers. It’s a modern horror classic that redefined what “helpless” feels like in a video game. ⭐ 9/10 – Play it with headphones, lights off, and a fresh pair of pants nearby.

208516117.review:
Outlast is a very creative horror game, and for me, the experience was amazing. There’s a lot of lore to uncover, and I did several playthroughs, including on Insane difficulty. I ended up getting all the achievements, yet I still crave more.

The camera mechanic is one of the most creative parts of the game. It’s a clever way to navigate dark areas and also includes a battery life system. On insane difficulty, aside from the scares, you’re always on high alert. Even if you know the game by heart, you stay tense and focused because one mistake in insane means game over, you’re sent back to the very beginning.

A few things I noticed: your character is actually faster than all enemies, and even if your battery runs out, you can still use the camera, that is nice touch since it still lets you collect notes.

However, the game isn’t perfect. It’s very linear, with few variations or alternate paths. After multiple playthroughs, it can start to feel repetitive, you rely more on memory than exploration or design. Sometimes you can get lost, but I think that’s fair for this type of game.

One thing I really disliked, though, is that you can occasionally get stuck in the environment. In one of my Insane runs (in Whistleblower), I got stuck in the geometry and had to start over from the very beginning since you can’t reload checkpoints on that difficulty. I think think it has to do more with Unreal Engine 3 than developer oversight.

Overall, Outlast is a horror game that everyone should experience at least once, including the Whistleblower DLC. It’s rich in lore through notes and documents. It also works for players who just want to dive straight into the horror. Whether you love story or pure horror, Outlast offers an unforgettable experience, one that will leave you traumatized in the best way possible. and that’s what makes it so special. Just a nice unique game.

208487067.review:
[h1] Game Premise: [/h1]
You are a investigative reporter named Upshur Miles, investigating the Mount Massive Asylum for suspicions of inhumane experiments taking place there, according to his anonymous source that works at the asylum. Once Upshur Miles gets inside the asylum, he gets stuck there while investigating the crazy things that are happening there, while also running, hiding and trying to escape.

[h1] Gameplay: [/h1]
The gameplay is in first-person perspective. A camera that helps you navigate in dark environments when night vision mode is turned on. To use night vision, you need batteries, which you find by exploring each area, along with documents that explain what happened in the asylum. You can record events or scenes with your camera; it automatically logs notes on whatever you just witnessed.
You can’t fight back against enemies. If you want to survive you either have to run away or hide from them.
The progression system is tied to the main character’s objectives: you must chase the required tasks, figure out where enemies are, and learn how to get past them. The gameplay loop keeps you tense. ready for the next encounter with questions like these: “When will the next scare happen? Is that enemy here? Where is the exit?” These questions add a subtle addictive quality to the fear.

[h1] Story: [/h1]
The story is engaging, but its impact drops if you skip reading the documents and notes scattered throughout. Those clues deepen the lore of what occurred at the asylum. The narrative and gameplay are interlinked: events can affect whether you have your camera, how fast you can run, etc.

[h1] Graphics & Visuals: [/h1]
At the time of it’s release it had great graphics and it looks really good even today. Realistic visuals enhance immersion, while the dark, gory tone contributes to an unsettling atmosphere. It’s simple mechanics further grounds the realism and immersion the game has to offer.
I didn’t encounter any performance issues on the platform that I played (Windows 11).

[h1] Sound & Music [/h1]
Sound effects contributes to the game’s atmosphere and immersion
The music in this game really heightens the atmosphere and the tension, especially when an enemy notices you.
Voice acting is solid: each enemy has a fitting voice, and the main character’s vocalizations like screams, breaths, etc. Are really effective even though he never speaks.

[h1] Replayability & Content: [/h1]
The game took me around 4-5 hours to beat.
There are 4 Difficulty Levels, the easiest being Normal Difficulty which was the difficulty i played it on, and the hardest being Insane in which the enemy is faster than you and also features permadeaths, if you die once you have to start over from the beginning.
It also has a story DLC that expands the main story, which I will cover in a separate review.

[h1] Pros: [/h1]
    • Great Story
    • Great Atmosphere
    • Great Soundtrack & Sound Effects
    • Good Graphics
    • Great Story DLC
    • Memorable Characters
    • Simple mechanics

[h1] Cons: [/h1]
    • Story loses a lot of depth if you don’t read the notes and documents.

[h1] Who Is This Game For: [/h1]
I would like to recommend this game, first and foremost to people who love horror/survival horror games, and to people who want to get into them.
I would also recommend it to people who watch horror movies and to those who can tolerate jumpscares and being in control of the character that is being chased.
I would not recommend it to people who don’t like horror/survival horror games and those who don’t tolerate too much gore, jumpscares, being chased.

[h1] Verdict: [/h1]
The game is a masterpiece in it’s genre. It’s simple gameplay mechanics combined with it’s compelling story, atmosphere and soundtrack make it one of the best horror games ever released. A must-play for Horror fans, and for those that want to dive in the horror genre of games.

[h1] 10/10: A Masterpiece. [/h1]

208158274.review:
Bought it today. Played it today. Finished it today.
And yeah, I’m still shaken.

Outlast scared the hell out of me. I’ve watched tons of horror movies and played “scary” games, but this one is different. No weapons. No fighting back. Just a camera, night vision, and panic. I thought I was hard to scare. I was wrong. I screamed more than once, no shame.

The atmosphere is insane. The asylum feels wrong in every possible way. The sounds, the lighting, the distant screams, even the silence makes you nervous. Half the time nothing is happening and you’re still terrified.

What really makes it work is the helplessness. You can only run, hide, and hope you’re not seen. The game never lets you relax. There’s always pressure, always fear.

It also holds up surprisingly well for its age. The pacing is great, it doesn’t overstay its welcome, and every section feels memorable. The story is darker than I expected too, especially if you actually read the notes.

About 5 hours in, I can safely say this is one of the best horror games I’ve ever played.
Short, intense, and unforgettable.

10/10.
I’ll replay it. Just not anytime soon.

Outlast is one of those games where you think,
“Eh, how scary can it be?” — and ten minutes later you’re crouched in a dark corner, camera flickering, whispering “please don’t find me” while regretting every life choice. 😂

The atmosphere is pure horror perfection.
You can’t fight back, only run or hide — and that makes every sound, every shadow, and every flicker of light absolutely terrifying.

Even after all these years, the graphics and sound design still hit hard.
Every hallway feels alive, every scream feels personal. It’s not just a game — it’s an experience that pushes your nerves to the limit and somehow keeps you coming back for more.

💀 Verdict:
Outlast is a masterpiece of horror – a perfect example of how fear, tension, and good design can make your pulse race without firing a single bullet.
Rating - 10/10⭐  – the ultimate heart-attack simulator, and I love it.

The atmosphere, soundtrack, and visuals are all excellent — they really set the tone and pull you in. However, once you get to the gameplay, things start to fall apart. At first, it’s engaging, but it quickly becomes repetitive and predictable. The loop is always the same: an enemy spots you, you run, hide under a bed or in a locker, wait, and repeat. There’s little variety or challenge, and it’s mostly just moving from point A to point B with no puzzles or deeper mechanics to keep things interesting.

The tension also fades fast — what’s scary at first soon turns into routine. Overall, this game would have made an amazing horror movie, but as a game, it loses its impact far too soon.

207594949.review:
I don't generally play survival horror games, but I thought I'd try this since I've heard of it and it was on sale in October. The game play is repetitive and relies on sound-enhanced jump scares, fetishistic body horror (good call not showing any women, but [i]that scene[/i] didn't need to be that long), and numerous chase sequences that take the game from unsettling to tedious. There's a specific path you have to take to escape each encounter, and you will survive if you take it, even if your pursuer is only a couple steps behind you. It's better to let yourself be killed and start over if you mess up, which gets old fast. It's great for achievement hunting but terrible for first time players without a walkthrough. 

The night vision mechanic with quickly dying batteries was relied on too heavily. I wish the sequence where you [spoiler] lost the camera [/spoiler] lasted longer. I also don't understand the logic of [spoiler] the walrider nanobot cloud showing up clearly with night vision but being nearly impossible to see otherwise.[/spoiler] The settings all blended together except the final chapter. I thought finding all the documents and notes would reveal an interesting story, but it was just a middling narrative dotted with a few interesting bits of real world information.

207513352.review:
This game saved my life.

I am 41.

My ex-wife and I have a daughter together, and adopted our son together. They are now both 4 years old.

When we were going through our separation, I found myself lost and miserable. I was self destructive. I got so mad one day from everything spiraling out of my control that I punched some concrete in a moment of overwhelming emotion. That caused me to break my 5th metacarpal in my right hand... my working hand... my games hand.. the hand that I held and carried my children to bed with.. The hand I desperately needed to make sure I could continue to provide.

After learning of the severity of my self-inflicted damage, I was borderline suicidal. Keep in mind that just a few months before this, I was the happiest man with no history of depression or anxiety. I have never had fits of rage, or been one to break down and cry, but I was in a low spot that just really buried me from being able to see the light on the other side.

Having nothing better to do, I searched for a game I could play, ONE HANDED while I recovered. I somehow stumbled upon this game and read some of the reviews. I decided that it had to be worth a shot... I must admit, I didn't beat the game, or play nearly as long as some of you. In fact, I may have only played this game a day or two.. With that being said, after doing so, I had a new found joy and hope for life. I was able to put behind me the pain and suffering that had been cast over me. I was able to experience other peoples joy and happiness. I was able to see the fruits of my "labor". I relaxed for 5 minutes to this music long enough to realize that I was going to be okay.
After coming to that realization, I turned the game off, and I went back to work. It hurt my hand like hell but I was motivated. I stopped feeling so damn sorry for myself, and I became the father I needed to be in that moment, not the weak boy I was behaving as.

Today, I am close friend with my kids mother. We don't fight, or argue, or say hurtful things to each other. We are parents, and friends.

I now have 3 kids. My third child is, wait for it, ALSO 4 YEARS OLD. The woman I am with was going through a very similar situation at the time of my own separation, and we just stumbled in to each others life unexpectedly. We have been in a relationship for a year now, and are very happy together.

Moral of the story, you never know what life holds in store for you, and if I would have given up when all the odds were stacked against me, I wouldn't be where I am today. This silly little game helped me realize that.

Thank you.

Alongside Amnesia, I would consider Outlast one of the grandfathers of modern survival horror and a must play for anyone who loves horror games. I'm glad this became a franchise that is still expanding because it had so much potential to do so just from this first game. Outlast could easily become another Silent Hill/Resident Evil level horror series.

205896098.review:
Outlast doesn’t ask you to fight—it dares you to survive. Set in the rotting belly of Mount Massive Asylum, it strips you of power and replaces it with a camcorder, night vision, and raw, suffocating fear. You’re not a hero; you’re prey. And that’s the point.

The game’s true horror isn't in its gore—though there’s plenty—but in its atmosphere. Every hallway hums with dread. The sound design breathes down your neck. The asylum is alive, not in a supernatural sense, but as a haunted monument to what happens when science forgets empathy.

204905722.review:
Outlast is survival horror at its purest, no weapons, no easy escapes, just you, a camcorder, and an asylum full of nightmares. From the first moment you step inside Mount Massive, the game masterfully builds tension through sound design, atmosphere, and moments of sheer panic that make you question if you’re brave enough to keep going.

The night-vision camera mechanic is brilliant, it forces you to balance exploration with resource management, and the fear of running out of batteries adds a constant layer of stress. The pacing is relentless: just when you think you’re safe, the game throws another heart-stopping chase or disturbing reveal at you.

Outlast isn’t just about jump scares (though it has plenty of those)—it’s about dread. Every hallway feels unsafe, every noise keeps you on edge, and every chase sequence has your pulse racing.

If you want a horror game that will truly test your nerves, Outlast delivers in every way. Just remember: you can’t fight back, you can only survive.

204379892.review: Very overrated, start off with that. Im a big fan of heavily story driven games, with environmental aspects. Stuff that tells the story, without stating, "this is the story!", all in your face. Games like Silent Hill do this well, and its not fair in my opinion to compare this game to SH, but Im doing so for you to understand what I mean. When playing Outlast, at the start I was absolutely horrified, to the point I could'nt even begin to play because I would get 20 mins in and quit. Finally I decided to suck the courage up and enjoy it, and I did! The first hour or 2 of Outlast was lots of fun! From then on however, the games flaws really began to stick out like a sore thumb. I believe I explored the areas a LOT. Trying to find secrets, or story beats. I felt as if I didnt find really anything. Nothing to keep me engaged in this mess of a story they are trying to tell. Unless you count the notes and documents, but even then I never had that "ohhh thats interesting", kind of 'in the head' moments. It all just felt like nonsense written to make you believe theres actually something important written down. When 90% of the time it was just like, "Patients have dreams and going crazy because of the SPOOKY TECH we are developing!". I felt as if every document was kind of just stating the same things over and over. A good word for this game is repetitive. From the gameplay becoming stale so fast, to the absolutely non-coherent story that feels as if you're going in circles, its just not great. I felt as if by the end of the game, none of my questions were answered, and none of the story was ever resolved. Infact, Im not exactly sure what story there even was to resolve. So many just weird things that never had a chance to be truly explained. which made me more confused then upset the game was over. Other than being the first, whats Billy's importance? What was the point of the priest? What even was that ending. So much nothing happened story wise in a game with so much going on. With my thoughts being stated, I still believe Outlast is a fun, campy horror game. The scares are not amazing, but they are not bad either. Its fun for what it is. Will I play it again? Maybe... probably not. Will I play the DLC again? Same answer. Maybe... but probably not. Its one of those 'one-and-done' type games, which does not make it bad. but not good either. Play it if your craving that horror game itch. skip it otherwise.

204116778.review: This is the best horror game I have ever played. The DLC is worth every penny. The sounds, the music, the places and locations, the atmosphere, the feeling of losing your mind with the main character to the images of pure atrocity, multiple enemies each with their own backstory and persona, and a secret that only gets deeper once you start digging into it, with no visible logical fallacies, and a non-generic climax point that does not leave you satisfied, but adds a layer of profoundness to it all (which makes you likely to purchase the DLC, in seek of more to the masterpiece you've already witnessed); this game has made a statement as heavy as can be on what indie game studios can and should be able to achieve. More than ten years later, most games of the same horror genre that make it in the top 100 seriously struggle to keep up with Red Barrels' work, which shows not only the expertise of the team, but also the fact that no matter the massive breakthroughs in technology and graphics, top-notch storytelling will never be outperformed, no matter the amusing amount of time put into making games look realistic by the developers of now. Outlast will never be outdated, and the story of Miles Upshur and Waylon Park - never topped.

203492140.review: The camcorder mechanic is one of Outlast’s most brilliant design choices. Its night vision doesn’t just let you see in the dark—it transforms the world into a grainy, claustrophobic nightmare where every shadow feels alive. The eerie green glow heightens the sense of dread, making even empty hallways feel oppressive. On top of that, the limited battery life adds a constant layer of anxiety. Every time you switch on night vision, you’re forced to ask yourself: Is this worth it? Should I conserve what little power I have left? That constant push and pull between survival and exploration makes every moment tense and unforgettable

203166759.review:
[h1] Disappointed. [/h1]

 [h3] First Half Of Outlast [/h3]

I downloaded this game because of the hype. Outlast is considered a classic horror title, I read the good reviews, watched the nice stories on youtube about it, and at first, I saw why. The opening is genuinely scary, the atmosphere is strong, and it feels like the game is guiding you clearly through the asylum. I had fun at the start for an old game such as this, it had some great graphics as well.

 [h3] Second Half of Outlast  [/h3]

Then comes the mad scientist chase. I ran around in circles for hours before giving up and looking up a walkthrough. Annoying, but forgivable. Unfortunately, everything after that falls apart. The game stops being scary and becomes pure frustration: constant dying, terrible AI that can sometimes even see you in the dark?! (Buddy i can’t see you, how the hell do you see me?!), unclear paths, and mechanics that punish you for no reason. Instead of fear, all I felt was throwing my mouse at the wall. Trial and error and error and error. There are mechanics that are genuinely broken sometimes. More than once even trying to climb on a ledge doesn’t work, just pushes me off over and over again, making me second-guess everytime if this is the right way. And.. okay. It’s dark. That’s great, it’s scary. But even opening flashlight blinds me sometimes. Sometimes things are so unbelievably hard to see, it’s no longer scary, just hurting my eyes from the squinting. 

The story also collapses by the end. It starts grounded and creepy, but [spoiler] then turns into weird supernatural ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that ruins the whole atmosphere. [/spoiler]  Watching YouTube lore videos made it sound deep and so good, actually playing it just made it feel cheap. Idk if this is because I lost fun in the horror and just was frustrated every minute by unclear instructions and paths that even the story feels like ♥♥♥♥ now. 

The first half of Outlast was fun, but the rest was a huge disappointment. 203158656.review: When I was around 9-10, I watched a lot of horror YouTube videos and of all the games I would watch lets players play, Outlast was the one that horrified me the most. I was so scared of outlast, I couldn't even stomach watching it for more than a few minutes in short snippets. I bought the game last year on sale, and it scared me so badly that I moved on to other games until very recently. While my ability to withstand horror grew significantly over that year and this game scared me much less, it will still a disgustingly horrifying experience. I have never seen "mentally insane" horror done better than outlast and never have had a setting disturb and make me feel less comfortable than Mount Massive Asylum. Part of what makes this game so unique beyond its setting is the night vision mechanic, not only does this stand out in a sea of flashlights, it also gives every dark section a disgusting green hue. The way everything blends into one color, how eyes glow in the dark and how limited the battery life is makes something as safe as "make a dark section bright" so much more uncomfortable than it tends to be. I personally am not too scared of chase sequences, however every part of this games atmosphere came together to make even short chase scenes feel so much more excited and horrifying than they'd be otherwise. I am incredibly happy to say that this game, while not the scariest experience once you start getting the end of the game, still holds up over 10 years later as a horror masterpiece and should be a star example of what indie horror can be. I would highly recommend Outlast to any horror fan who for some reason has yet to play one of the most defining games in the genre.

202763534.review:
[code] This is just the author's opinion. The game has been completed with 100% achievements. [/code]

[h1] Is the 2013 horror film Outlast still relevant? [/h1]
[b] I finally got around to writing a relevant review for 2025 (August 24th) about a survival horror game called Outlast! Yes, having played through the game multiple times, collected all the documents and notes, and learned the full plot... I can now confidently state my thoughts on it! [/b]

[h3] About the plot [/h3]
[b] We're getting to know our main character, Miles Upshur, an investigative journalist. He's committed to Mount Massive Asylum, but for what purpose and how? You'll find out from the first document you're given at the beginning. I think the developers did a great job of laying the groundwork for understanding what's going on right from the start! Let's move on to how the plot is presented! If you've guessed, the plot is presented through documents and a few notes from our journalist. [spoiler] ( As you play, you'll understand how to make recordings, just keep recording)  [/spoiler]
Once you get to the Mental Hospital, oh... you're in for some fun, jump scares, and recordings FROM THE VERY BEGINNING... so stock up on batteries and [strike] spare underwear [/strike] ! After all, you'll be given a warm welcome, everyone here will be hospitable to you, and if you accidentally get hit on the forehead with a baton... consider it a greeting! Which you can't respond to, so, with a grain of truth, they'll start beating you up further. Our journalist is literally faced with the fact that everyone wants him [strike] kill [/strike], Moreover, he encounters various psychos driven mad by experiments on them. The only one who receives us is Priest Martin, who will then guide you throughout the hospital and make sure you recognize this place. [/b]

[h3]Gameplay and horror components [/h3]
[b]The game's mechanics are fairly simple. As you progress, the game will tell you where to poke your paws. We didn't notice any bugs, bad textures, or anything like that. As for the game itself, you won't encounter the problem of running like crazy to escape those who want you [strike] kill [/strike], And with the fact that your fears and anxiety will take over. You'll often feel as if you're being watched, trapped, and even more so, isolated, as the location—color, architecture—doesn't change. You'll experience a fast-paced development, changing locations where you'll be able to take a break and take a walk, while others will haunt you. And last but not least, it's dark everywhere, so be prepared to constantly stock up on batteries...[/b]

[h3]Content and graphics [/h3]
[b]The game offers a fair amount of content, including 18+ content and an interesting DLC ​​worth playing! Guts, genitals, hearts, kidneys, and stuffed corpses await you in this game, so prepare yourself well to avoid vomiting! As for the graphics, the location itself, the multitude of objects, the architecture itself, and, no less, the characters, particularly our character's body, are very frightening and well-designed! Of the details, I'll highlight the characters' actions; the developers didn't bother with them screaming, banging their heads, pacing, having their own lines, making "love" with others, or even with a corpse... For this, I give it a huge, fat PLUS, because it's alarming and makes you wary, keeping an eye out for attacks![/b]

[h3]Pros [/h3]
- High-quality graphics.
- Your tasks are shown to you.
- Highlighting of important objects.
- Easy to set up.

[h3] Cons [/h3]
[b]One downside is the limited presentation of important dialogue with Priest Martin, who could have explained more of the plot. After playing through the game several times and collecting all the folders in the main game and the DLC, the plot remained unquestioned for me. So, the main idea is: if you want to fully understand the story, play on normal difficulty and explore the locations completely. The game allows you to do so. Read the documents carefully, and you'll be even more intrigued as you progress and understand the story.[/b]

[h3] Pure opinion and experience [/h3]
The game is quite scary, but once you've played it through a few times, it's not so scary anymore; it's less stressful. As for the achievements, they don't take long (I personally just finished the game on full achievements), but you'll encounter challenges at times on the "Psycho" (or "madness") difficulty level, as sometimes you won't be able to complete it on your first try because so many characters will shoot you. So, from me personally, for achievement-hunters... Prepare for torture! Use guides often, review what goes where, why, and how before important moments... you'll get through it in one breath!

[h3] Conclusion: The game is recommended for playing as an experience in Survival Horror! [/h3]

I thought I could handle Outlast, but I was wrong. From the moment I stepped into Mount Massive Asylum, the atmosphere crushed me. Every hallway felt like it was watching me, every sound made my skin crawl. The darkness wasn’t just around me—it was inside me.

The worst part? You can’t fight back. All you have is a camera with night vision, and instead of comfort, it only makes the shadows even more terrifying. I kept telling myself I’d push further, but each jump scare drained me until I couldn’t go on.

I didn’t finish Outlast. I couldn’t. It’s not just a game—it’s a nightmare that follows you even after you turn it off.

201866899.review:
This is a certified spooky game. I was genuinely scared and uncomfortable for my first playthrough. In fact, I had to take a break from the game for a few days in order to process and handle the horror. This is a fantastic horror experience and I would highly recommend it. However, I have one major gripe with the game, unskipable cutscenes.

Now, these cutscenes are not an issue for 90% of playthroughs. However, If you are attempting the lunatic achievement (beating the game on the permadeath difficulty), these unskipable cutscenes get old, fast. The first chapter alone has upwards of 10 minutes of watching cutscenes. This is normally not an issue but when you have to rewatch them every single run, you get sick of it. The worst one however, appears later in the game. In the [spoiler]Male ward where trager ties you to the wheelchair, you have to watch the 5 minute cutscene until you can move again[/spoiler]. All these cutscenes make you understand why the difficulty is called "insane". By the end of it, you will have lost all your sanity.

Besides that, this game is fantastic and I would recommend it to any horror fans.

8/10

201050499.review:
Outlast is a terrifying and intense survival horror game that redefined the genre with its raw atmosphere, relentless pacing, and complete sense of helplessness. Set in the eerie halls of Mount Massive Asylum, the game immerses you in a nightmare where your only tools are a camcorder and your ability to run and hide.

From the moment you step inside the asylum, Outlast wastes no time plunging you into madness. The game's first-person perspective heightens the fear, and the night vision mechanic — powered by your camcorder’s limited battery — adds a layer of constant tension. You're always scanning the darkness, never sure what’s waiting just beyond your field of view.

The atmosphere is oppressive and masterfully designed. Blood-smeared walls, flickering lights, disturbing audio, and grotesque visuals make every corridor feel alive and dangerous. The enemy AI is ruthless, forcing you into frantic chase sequences where every decision matters.

What sets Outlast apart is its refusal to empower the player. There are no weapons, no combat — just stealth, speed, and fear. This vulnerability makes every encounter feel genuinely threatening, creating a level of immersion few horror games can match.

The story, told through found documents and terrifying discoveries, explores themes of corruption, madness, and the abuse of science and faith. It's dark, disturbing, and thought-provoking — everything a psychological horror should be.

In summary, Outlast is a horror masterpiece. It’s brutally scary, brilliantly paced, and endlessly intense. If you’re looking for a horror game that pushes your nerves to the edge and keeps you there, Outlast delivers in every way.

List (folium, fylom) je postranní vegetativní zelený orgán rostlin. Je omezeného vzrůstu[zdroj?] (dosahuje velikosti od několika milimetrů až po 2 metry čtvereční) a zpravidla do plochy rozšířený. Plošné rozšíření listu je evolučně výhodná adaptace rostlinného těla, která umožňuje rostlině efektivnější zachytávání světla listy, tato efektivita pak může být ještě umocněna aktivním nastavením listu vůči světelným paprskům. Listy dělíme na asimilační listy (základní typ), dále děložní lístky a listeny. List má tři základní funkce – výměna plynů s okolním prostředím, fotosyntéza a odpařování vody. Pokud je sucho, tak opadávají, aby zabránily ztrátám potřebné vody.[1]

200101236.review:
Outlast - An Entertaining Freak Show

Outlast is an almost-perfect horror game. It managed to be a pioneer for the "Walking-Sim" subgenre of Horror Games where most of the action is you walking around and hiding. But unlike most of its contemporaries, Outlast is unique and sticks out with just how sharply-focused it is in delivering its story to you.

This game made me feel incredibly uncomfortable and uneasy throughout thanks to how it handles its subject matter and how it shows it to you.

If a story fails from "Tell, don't show", this story thrives on "Show, don't tell". None of the information in this game is fed to you. You, yourself, will discover the horrors of just what goes wrong in this Asylum.
You will witness death, gore, sexual deviancy, naked men with no pants chasing you through hallways and crevices... It is all there and you will witness it, all the way from beginning to end.

If you enjoy Horror Games. You will not go wrong with this title, my review is just one of the many others that add to the "Overwhelmingly Positive" rating, and none of what I say is in any way original and has probably been said before.

Yes there is some jank, yes there is some trial and error. But it is worth sticking throuh, especially when buying this title on a sale.

Don't overlook this gem.

200041507.review:
I would describe this as one of the best horror games around. 

You are a journalist who is investigating a tip that some batshiz crazy stuff is going down at an asylum, located deep within mountains of Colorado. You are to get in, and document what is happening inside. You get inside and all hell is unleashed on you, the people inside have gone mad, and a fair amount are driven to try kill you upon sight. You use your trusty camcorder to record the events, and to see in the dark via night vision.

The atmosphere of this game is absolutely terrifying, and bleak. It ranks up there with Manhunt (2003) , and Condemned Criminal Origins (2005) of which have a similar feel. Though in those games your character can fight.

In Outlast, your character can't fight, which makes you feel helpless, and somehow (I swear this is a good thing for this game) miserable. There will be times where upon being pursued you are frozen in fear, hiding under a bed or in a locker, with some of the scariest sound design I have ever heard. This could leave many scared, or frustrated as to what the next move should be, all while using night vision that could run out via battery, leaving you to use a weaker night vision, or darkness if you so choose ( a fun choice to keep the game scary, searching for batteries is unnerving too).  There are corpses and gore everywhere in this game, leaving you to only guess how scary it was before arriving at the asylum. The few people who wont kill you are only there to add context or atmosphere, of which is scary or unnerving as well.

The game consists of hiding and not being caught, and finding batteries for your camera. That's it. No bottles or diversions for enemies, just the occasional door slam that might slow them down, if and when you are being pursued. 

This is true horror down to its core, and isn't too difficult to complete. If you can get past the fear of being caught by the enemies, some occasional trial and error design, and quite a few jumpscares ( i think they are tolerable), then this is a masterpiece of the genre. The graphics and sound look good for a game that's getting a bit older as well. 

The enemies in this game (Chris Walker, I'm looking at you!) are memorable and very intimidating. The story is unsettling, and evokes real world horror and conspiracy theories. 

Id say get this, and Outlast 2 on sale, mainly to have them both for a good price. Outlast Trials is also excellent to play alone or with friends.

I give this game an 8.5/10

199796210.review:
If you're looking for a truly terrifying experience, Outlast delivers horror in its purest form. Developed by Red Barrels, Outlast is not just a game—it's a nerve-shredding psychological journey that will keep you on edge from start to finish.

Set in the ominous Mount Massive Asylum, you play as investigative journalist Miles Upshur, armed with nothing but a camcorder. That’s right—no weapons, no combat—just your wits, your ability to run, and your courage (which will be tested constantly). The use of night vision through the camcorder is genius; it not only enhances immersion but also heightens the tension, especially when your battery starts dying in the pitch-black corridors.

The atmosphere is masterfully crafted. The sound design is haunting, with every creak, scream, and breath making your skin crawl. Visually, the asylum is disturbing and detailed, filled with grotesque imagery and a constant sense of dread. The game doesn’t rely on cheap jump scares (though there are a few) but builds genuine psychological horror through storytelling, pacing, and environmental detail.

What makes Outlast stand out is its ability to make the player feel completely powerless. You can’t fight back—your only options are to hide or run. This mechanic makes every encounter feel life-threatening, creating a constant adrenaline rush.

Pros:

Incredibly immersive horror atmosphere

Excellent sound design and visuals

Unique game play mechanics (no combat)

Gripping story with disturbing lore

Smooth performance on PC

Cons:

May be too intense for some players

Relatively short playtime (6–8 hours)

Final Verdict:
Outlast is a must-play for horror fans. It's disturbing, unforgettable, and expertly crafted. If you dare to enter Mount Massive Asylum, prepare for sleepless nights and heart-pounding moments you won't soon forget.

199792451.review:

9/10 story and lore

199235207.review:
First off, I never write reviews. For me to go out of my way to type this should mean that this game series is something special. I know I'm late to the party of this game, but what made me check it out was falling in love with Outlast Trials and the quality of that game. So, in the Steam Summer Sale 2025, I bought the entire Red Barrels catalog. I'm a horror movie fan, but never really enjoyed playing horror games that much due to them usually being cheap in quality and the spooks. However, this series really left a mark on me, and I'm glad I bravely tried it and went out of my comfort zone. This game is the peak horror gaming experience. 

First off, this game's setting and story are really compelling and leave you with dreadful curiosity as to what similar things are out there and have been done in real life. The gameplay is nothing but running and hiding, but the atmosphere and darkness alone can be the main antagonist at times, making you feel a sense of terror even when there is no big bad chasing you or hunting you. The camera night vision adds so much to this game that it's almost laughable how well it works, given its simplicity. 

The characters that you meet, or shall I say, survive throughout this game, have been some of the most memorable things in gaming for me. From the big pig guy to Mr. Scissor Hands, there are so many interesting personalities going on here, and I feel that is one of the things Red Barrel does best. There is no holding back on the dark and gruesome elements of these monsters. 

Also, I never achievement hunt in games, it tends to be too tedious and frustrating, and seems to be a waste of my time mostly. However, I wanted any excuse to go back and play this game again and give myself a little encore of dopamine. Ive 100% this game and plan on doing this for the rest of them. 6/10 on the difficulty part of achieving this, so it's not that bad. 

Please do yourself a favor and buy this game and the DLC if it's on sale, OR NOT. It's so worth getting immersed in this universe, and you will not regret spending your time on the Murkoff Corporation grounds. This game is insanely well paced, and my first playthrough of the main game, non-DLC, took almost 3 hours. DLC is a little shorter, and the only thing I wish is that there was more content. 10/10 experience, and Red Barrel is now on my radar as a must-buy gaming company.

199044724.review:
Outlast Review – A Terrifying Classic That Still Delivers
I played Outlast for around 7 hours, completing both the main storyline and its DLC, which ties up the narrative beautifully. Outlast, in my opinion, is one of the top names in the horror genre, and for good reason. The atmosphere is masterfully crafted—the oppressive environments, chilling lighting, and iconic night-vision camera mechanics come together to create a deeply immersive experience.

The fear in this game is genuine and effective. If it's your first time playing, there’s no doubt you’ll be genuinely surprised and frightened multiple times. The game uses audio and visuals intelligently to build tension, and the sound design is one of its strongest elements—tense, haunting, and highly immersive.

The gameplay mechanics, especially the stealth and escape sequences, never felt repetitive. There’s a good level of variation that keeps things fresh, and while some of the enemies feel a bit copy-pasted (especially the generic police-type NPCs), it’s a minor flaw in an otherwise memorable game. Character variety is the one weak point—it becomes noticeable when the same enemy models appear repeatedly.

The storyline leans towards the predictable side with a few clichés, but it remains satisfying enough to keep you engaged, especially with the DLC that adds important narrative closure.

If you're a fan of horror, Outlast is an essential experience. It’s not just scary—it’s atmospherically intense, mechanically sound, and visually iconic. I’d give it an 8 out of 10 within the horror genre, and absolutely recommend it to any fan of psychological and survival horror games.

198804730.review:
I don’t care what anyone says — Outlast isn’t a game. It’s a psychological torture device disguised as entertainment. You don’t play Outlast. You survive Outlast. Barely. And even that’s pushing it.

I fired this ♥♥♥♥ up thinking I was some kind of horror game veteran. You know, big balls, no flashlight needed, full volume. Ten minutes in, I was hiding in a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ locker, whispering to myself like a prisoner of war. That camcorder night vision? Yeah, that’s not a cool mechanic — it’s a panic-inducing “how much battery do I have before I die” simulator. I ran out of batteries and nearly cried.

This game is in a league of its own. It doesn’t hold your hand. It slaps it, dislocates your shoulder, and throws you headfirst into a psychiatric hellhole full of naked dudes trying to murder you with rebar. Every single sound — a door creaking, a pipe hissing — felt like Satan breathing on my neck.

And the chase scenes? Bro. BRO. When you hear that music kick in and some half-dead lunatic starts sprinting after you like a police officer chasing a black guy? I alt-F4’d so fast I nearly broke my keyboard.

I had to refund it. Not because it was bad — it’s TOO good. My fight-or-flight response is permanently stuck on flight.

Do I recommend it? ♥♥♥♥ yes. Just not to me. Or anyone with a functioning nervous system.

"Outlast is Pure Terror — and I Loved Every Second of It!" 

Outlast is one of the most intense and horrifying survival horror games I’ve ever played. From the moment you enter Mount Massive Asylum, the atmosphere grabs you and doesn’t let go. The sound design, the lighting, the pacing—it’s all expertly crafted to keep your nerves on edge.

 The enemies aren’t just monsters. They’re characters with disturbing stories, which makes encounters so much more chilling.
 The camcorder night vision mechanic? Brilliant. It made me second-guess every step I took.
 No combat. Just run, hide, and hope. That helplessness makes everything hit harder.

I documented my full play through on YouTube—every scream, every close call, every sprint-for-your-life moment. If you're curious how the fear plays out in real time, or want to relive it before diving in yourself, check out the full series here:

If you’re into survival horror that doesn’t hold back, Outlast is a must-play. Just... maybe don’t play it alone in the dark.

198584800.review:
[h1]Try to Outlast the Intense Horror in an Asylum of Insanity[/h1]

[i]Hell is an experiment you can't survive in Outlast, a first-person survival horror game developed by veterans of some of the biggest game franchises in history. As investigative journalist Miles Upshur, explore Mount Massive Asylum and try to survive long enough to discover its terrible secret... if you dare.[/i]

[h2]Story[/h2]
You play as Miles Upshur, an investigative journalist who receives an anonymous tip about unethical experiments being conducted at the Mount Massive Asylum, a remote psychiatric hospital in the mountains of Colorado, operated by the Murkoff Corporation.

[h2]Gameplay[/h2]
Outlast is a first-person survival horror game. It emphasizes sneaking, exploration, and survival, with no combat mechanics. The only thing you have at your disposal is a camcorder, with night vision which you have to use frequently. It consumes batteries, adding resource management pressure. You don’t want to be stumbling in the dark with no battery when chased by crazy inmates, so deciding when it’s worth using night vision and when to conserve power is crucial. Survival relies entirely on running, hiding, and avoiding enemies.

[h2]Environment[/h2]
Various inmates and psychopaths, and the Walrider.

[h2]Environment & Scares[/h2]
The entire game takes place inside and around this remote psychiatric hospital. It has a good and creepy atmosphere, where you feel like sinister enemies are lurking around every corner. The game focuses a lot more on panic induced moments than cheap jump scares. You really don’t want to be seen by the enemies as they are quite fast. 

[h2]Visual Design & Sound Design[/h2]
The visual design is good for a game from that time. The visual design of the asylum and enemies are well made. The game focuses its visuals and sounds on psychological horror. Rooms are renovated for the purpose of conducting experiments on inmates. Blood, gore, and decay are everywhere - walls painted in blood, corpses in wheelchairs, and body parts scattered in sinks, scratched-in messages on walls, and crude symbols and altars set up by deranged patients. The enemies are grotesquely designed, with surgical scars, filthy and deformed faces, and mutilated limbs. All this adds to the immersion and you can almost taste the insanity on the tip of your tongue.

The background audio is constantly unsettling, filled with distant screams, echoing footsteps, moaning, crying, or whispering from unseen patients, wind rattling windows, pipes hissing, or lights buzzing. Sound is important, so you know when enemies are near. It also questions your sanity about what is real and what is not.

[h2]Constructive Criticism[/h2]
The transition to the sci-fi elements (the Walrider and nanotechnology) might feel weird for many players expecting a purely psychological or religious horror story. I personally did not like this approach or the gameplay against the Walrider.

Certain enemies (like Chris Walker) show up so often they begin to feel less scary, more like obstacles than threats.

The enemy AI was intimidating at first, but can be easily manipulated once you understand the mechanics. If there are two lockers in a room, the enemies will always check the one you are not in, unless they saw you hide.

The game is all about running and hiding. There are no puzzles or other interaction with the world. And after learning how to exploit enemy AI, the game might feel repetitive for some. I never felt this was an issue, since the game is fairly short.

[h2]The Verdict[/h2]
Outlast is an intense horror experience that scared me real good when it came out. The AI can be exploited, and the lack of gameplay variety may lead to fatigue for some, though its relatively short length helps prevent overstaying its welcome. It thrives on atmosphere, panic, and psychological fears. The grotesque visuals and disturbing sound effects add a lot to the immersion. The game holds up well even today. I strongly recommend it since it’s cheap and often on sale.

[h2]Rating: 8/10[/h2]

198538300.review:
Ah yes, Outlast. The game where you, an unarmed investigative journalist with the upper body strength of a baguette, decide it's a good idea to break into an abandoned asylum at night — with a camcorder. Not a gun. Not a flashlight. A ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ camcorder.

You spend most of your time filming things you absolutely shouldn't be filming, breathing louder than an asthmatic hippo, and sprinting like a toddler who just got told “no.” If you’ve ever wanted to roleplay as a scared intern stuck in a haunted nightmare with the cardio of a marathon runner, this is the game for you.

The controls are tight, the graphics are impressive, and the audio? Oh, the audio. Expect creaking, screaming, your own heartbeat, and the voice in your head asking, “Why didn’t I just play Stardew Valley?”

Battery life is so realistic it hurts. You’ll start treating AA batteries like sacred artifacts. Every time you pick one up, it feels like divine intervention.

In conclusion:
A+ horror experience.
D– life choices.

Outlast is such a good old classic for me. I played it a long time ago, but I recently came back to it and I have to say that the game is still worth playing. The atmosphere is amazing, really suspenseful from start to finish. It's one of those horror games that really scares you, not just with some cheap scares, but that suffocating atmosphere where you never know what's around the corner. The Whistleblower DLC was also a very pleasant surprise - perhaps even more psycho than the base game. I liked that it showed the background of what was actually going on in that sanitarium.

Overall, Outlast is a great horror game for me. If anyone hasn't played it yet, I definitely recommend it. And if you've already played it - it's worth giving it another chance.

The only minus that I would perhaps criticize is the stereotype of chasing and hiding

Well, what can I say about this game?

It's so good that made me like horror games, it rose the bar so high... also it was so enjoyable, apart from the extremely scary that it can get, that completed the main story and the DLC in Insane mode and got all the achievements.

The game doesn't shine for its plot or gameplay, except for the camera that makes it special and part of the game personality, it's normal and average first-person mobility and interactions.
Outlast put us in a abandoned asylum where "strange" things happen and your mission is, in first place, to find out what is happening and/or who is behind all of that, but then it turns around and it becomes a live or death matter to survive and get out, unfortunately it won't be easy.
 
The atmosphere is really, really good, the sounds, characters, enemies, collectibles, etc. There was not a single time where I felt safe or relaxed, Red Barrel did a really good job whit that.

I definitely recommend it as well as the DLC "Whistleblower". Just played 1 or 2 hours of the second game but it looks rally good, better looking, more scary and really promising.

Only one thing that was really bad is that when I finished insane mode whit the "Pulitzer" achievement, it only gave me that achievement but not "Legacy", the one for the completing all the recordings. 

Idk what could have happened but that's it. 

Give it a shot. Nice game.

196822444.review: Outlast has not held up but that's to be expected. It is very reliant on jump scares and body horror. The dated graphics makes the body horror part not that scary. It has some very repetitive gameplay: Turn two valves and hit a button while dodging somebody patrolling the area. I eventually learned that as long as they don't see you entering a locker they will search the one next to you then leave.  I like the design of the asylum, the game has some good atmosphere. There is not a lot of music but what's there is good. Miles breathing adds a lot to the tension. I like the way the game looks when you are using the night vision on your camera. It has become the most iconic part of the game. I really wish they would limit the film grain to only be on when using the camera. Trager is the most memorable character, he has some good dialogue. The chase sequence leading up to his reveal is probably the best chase in the main game. Chris Walker has some good lore but is overused imo. I love how he rips your entire body from your head if he catches you. There is some interesting lore if you care to read the documents. The game is pretty short if you know where to go. One of the best areas of the game is when you lose your camera. One of the worst is the ending lab. Exposition dump followed by a really boring ghost chase sequence. Even though it shows its age I have a soft spot for the game because it was one of the first horror games I played.

196262564.review:
So I do enjoy this game, but I don't necessarily recommend it. Let's talk about it...

I've played outlast about five times now. Both on Xbox and PC. While a lot of reviews mention bugs, I didn't experience any aside from one instance recently where when I skipped the post-game credits, my entire PC froze. Not fun but easily fixable. Anyway, pros and cons:

Pros: 
- I find this game quite scary. I hate being chased and that is the main element of this game, being chased and hiding. The enemies are all quite freaky looking and violent. There's blood and guts everywhere (not for the faint of heart).
- I think the setting for the game is a 10/10, the ambience and music absolutely contribute to player anxiety and tension. There's absolutely zero repose. The environments in each level of the game are dark, disgusting, and unsettling.
- There's some pretty solid jump-scares scattered throughout.
- You get a decent amount of game play for $20, 3-7 hours I'd say depending on how long it takes you and if you play the DLC or not. Although I do recommend getting the game on sale, not sure it's worth full price.


Cons:
- You can't fight back. All you can do is run or hide and I despise that.
- This game doesn't have a single ounce of actual "game play" in it, it is literally (as the other negative reviews have so bluntly put) a glorified walking simulator. You progress from point to point, avoiding the enemies whilst completing checkpoints to advance. (ex. you need to unlock the main doors, but oh no the generator needs to be turned back on. but oh no, to turn on the generator you have to activate two levers. in a dark cellar with a psycho hunting you.) it gets frustrating pretty fast.
- It's quite graphic. I would never blindly recommend this game to anyone. It's brutally gory and triggering, although not nearly as brutal as the second game (but that's a whole other review). 
- Once you've played it, the replay-ability factor is weak. You can predictably anticipate the enemies movements and actions, you know where the jump scares are, and it's not scary anymore, just irritating. I can't see myself playing the game again for years. 
- I found the plot interesting at first, I love a good abandoned asylum and some psychos. However, and SPOILER ALERT HERE, the ending is insanely underwhelming. All the crazy ♥♥♥♥ you've experienced is all dumbed down to the source: a ghost? You mean to tell me after all the experimentation, the mutated enemies/"variants", the poor patients who've been mutilated and tortured, the true enemy/boss fight (if you can even call it that) is a ghost? Man is that ever disappointing, the end of the game isn't even scary. You're being chased by something that doesn't even physically manifest. It felt like watching a hallmark movie I can't lie. 

All in all it's not a terrible game, it's just not for everyone. I always suggest people watch a little bit of game play on youtube or something before buying just to make sure it's something they can stomach, and that it's something they'd actually have fun playing through. And if you must play it, try to get it on sale.

195941100.review: Outlast is a great horror game that really knows how to freak you out. It has this creepy vibe right from the start, with visuals that really set the feeling that something’s wrong. You spend most of the time in tight, rundown hallways and rooms with worn-down walls, broken lights, and flickering bulbs, which makes everything feel super real and intense. The blood stains and other creepy details add to the horror and can tell their own little story about what went down there. The night vision on your camera is a lifesaver. It lets you see in almost total darkness, but the vision is limited and grainy, which just adds to the tension and makes everything feel even scarier. Sometimes it’s a bit confusing figuring out where to go, but the visuals really make it feel like you’re right there in all the scary stuff. It’s pretty intense and creepy, and honestly, totally worth grabbing.

Do I recommend it? YES.
Outlast is pure horror. One of the scariest games I’ve ever played. And one of my personal favorites, If you like adrenaline, this one’s for you.

PROS:

The atmosphere is insane. Dark, intense, and claustrophobic in the best way.

The camera mechanic with night vision is genius, it makes everything feel 10x scarier.

No weapons. You’re completely defenseless, which makes running and hiding super intense.

The sound design? 10/10. Every step, scream, and whisper keeps you on edge.

The story is disturbing but really interesting if you pay attention.

CONS:

It’s a bit short. You can finish it in one or two sittings.

Not for the faint of heart... seriously, if you don’t like being scared, stay away.

CONCLUSION:
10/10. A terrifying, unforgettable experience.

195473464.review:
Outlast isn’t just a horror game—it’s a full-blown nightmare brought to life, and one of the most effective psychological horror experiences in modern gaming. You play as an investigative journalist lured into a derelict insane asylum by the promise of a story, only to find yourself trapped in a living hell with no weapons, no safety, and no real escape. Armed with only a night-vision camera, you’re forced to witness horrors that you'd give anything to unsee.

What makes Outlast so unforgettable isn’t just the grotesque imagery or brutal jump scares—it’s the constant, choking atmosphere of dread. The Mount Massive Asylum is more than a setting; it’s a malevolent force in its own right. Flickering lights, disembodied screams, and distant footsteps all conspire to keep you in a constant state of tension. Even when nothing is chasing you, Outlast makes you feel hunted.

The sound design is nothing short of masterful. Whether it’s the low buzz of static from your camera, the echo of a scream down a blood-smeared hallway, or the sickening thud of something just out of sight, the game uses audio to unnerve and unbalance you at every turn. You’re never really sure when you’re safe—and that’s exactly the point.

What’s more impressive is how Outlast builds its fear not just through scares, but through vulnerability. There are no weapons here, no way to fight back. Your only option is to run or hide. And when the game turns up the intensity, those moments of desperate flight—heart pounding, camera battery dying—are some of the most terrifying you’ll ever play.

Surprisingly, the game also tells a story that’s deeper than expected. What begins as a typical "escape the asylum" plot slowly mutates into something far darker and stranger. If you take the time to dig into the documents and environmental storytelling, a twisted logic starts to emerge from the chaos. By the time you reach the finale, it’s no longer just about surviving—it’s about reckoning with what you’ve become.

Outlast also avoids the pitfall of overplaying its hand. It paces its scares well, letting moments breathe just enough before tightening the noose again. And although it doesn’t lean on complex mechanics, everything in the game is well utilized—from the battery management of your camera to the hide-and-seek encounters with horrifying enemies.

This is a horror game that gets under your skin. It makes you care about the protagonist, even when you're mostly watching him get emotionally and physically wrecked. And while it’s not perfect, it is laser-focused on what it wants to do—and it executes that vision brilliantly.

Whether you're a seasoned horror fan or just looking to be scared in a deeply psychological way, Outlast is essential. It’s not just scary—it’s disturbingly effective. And once you enter Mount Massive, part of you never quite leaves.
